questions.question: If the coefficient of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(a x^2+b x+c\right)(1-2 x)^{26}$ is -56 and the coefficients of $x^2$ and $x^3$ are both zero, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \left(a x^2+b x+c\right)(1-2 x)^{26} \\ & a x^2(1-2 x)^{26}+b x(1-2 x)^{26}+c(1-2 x)^{26} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Coefficient of }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x \Rightarrow\left[0+b \cdot{ }^{26} C_0+c \cdot{ }^{26} C_1(-2)\right] x \\ & =b-52 c \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Coefficient of

$$ \begin{aligned} & x^2=\left(a\left({ }^{26} C_0\right)+b \cdot{ }^{26} C_1(-2)+c{ }^{26} C_2(-2)^2\right) x^2 \\ & =a-52 b+{ }^{26} C_2 \times 4=a-52 b+1300 c \end{aligned} $$

Coefficient of

$$ \begin{aligned} & x^3=\left[a \cdot{ }^{26} C_1(-2)+b \cdot{ }^{26} C_2(26)^2+c^{26} C_3(-2)^3\right] \\ & =-52 a+1300 b-20800 c \\ & \Rightarrow b-52 c=-56 \\ & \Rightarrow b-52 c=-56 \\ & \begin{aligned} a-52 b+1300 c=0 \quad \Rightarrow & a=1300 \\ \quad-a+25 b-400 c=0 \quad & b=100 \\ & c=3 \\ \Rightarrow & a+b+c=1403 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

questions.question: The sum of all possible values of $\mathbf{n} \in \mathbf{N}$, so that the coefficients of $x, x^2$ and $x^3$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x^2\right)^2(1+x)^{\mathrm{n}}$, are in arithmetic progression is :
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+x^2\right)^2\left(1+x^n\right) \\ & \left(1+x^4+2 x^2\right)(1+x)^n \\ & \text { Coeff of } x={ }^n C_1 \\ & \text { Coeff of } x^2={ }^n C_2+2 \cdot{ }^n C_0 \\ & \text { Coeff of } x^3={ }^n C_3+2 \cdot{ }^n C_1 \\ & \because \text { Coeff of } x, x^2, x^3 \rightarrow \mathrm{AP} \\ & \Rightarrow 2\left({ }^n C_2+2\right)={ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_3+2 n \\ & 2^n C_2+4={ }^n C_3+3 n \\ & \Rightarrow n^2-9 n^2+26 n-24=0 \\ & \Rightarrow n=2,3,4 \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Sum of all value of } n \text { is } 9 \end{aligned} $$

questions.question: Let $\mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{25!}+\frac{1}{3!23!}+\frac{1}{5!21!}+\ldots$ up to 13 terms. If $13 \mathrm{~S}=\frac{2^k}{n!}, k \in \mathrm{~N}$, then $n+k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$ S=\frac{1}{1!25!}+\frac{1}{3!23!}+\frac{1}{5!21!}+\ldots 13 \text { terms } $$

multiply in numerator and denominator by 26 !

$$ \begin{aligned} & S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[\frac{26!}{1!25!}+\frac{26!}{3!23!}+\frac{26!}{5!2!}+\ldots 13 \text { term }\right] \\ & S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[{ }^{26} C_1+{ }^{26} C_3+{ }^{26} C_5+\ldots 13 \text { term }\right],\left\{{ }^n C_r=\frac{n!}{(n-r)!r!}\right\} \end{aligned} $$

Lets find 13th term lower subscript are in A.P with, common difference $=2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & T_{13}=1+(13-1) 2=25 \\ & \text { so, } S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[{ }^{26} C_1+{ }^{26} C_3+{ }^{26} C_5+\ldots{ }^{26} C_{25}\right] \\ & S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[2^{26-1}\right],\left\{\text { because }{ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_3+{ }^n C_5 \cdots=2^{n-1}\right\} \end{aligned} $$

$$ S=\frac{2^{25}}{26!} $$

It is given that $13 S=\frac{2^K}{n!}$

substitute value of $S$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 13 \times \frac{2^{25}}{26!}=\frac{2^K}{n!} \\ & \frac{13 \times 2^{25}}{26 \times 25!}=\frac{2^K}{n!} \\ & \frac{2^{24}}{25!}=\frac{2^K}{n!} \end{aligned} $$

so $n=25, K=24$

$n+K=25+24=49$

questions.question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I :

$25^{13} + 20^{13} + 8^{13} + 3^{13}$ is divisible by 7.

Statement II :

The integral part of $(7 + 4\sqrt{3})^{25}$ is an odd number.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
questions.solution:
Statement I : $25^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by 7.

Key concept : If $n$ is odd, $x^n+y^n=(x+y)\left(x^{n-1}-x^{n-2} y+x^{n-3} y^2-\cdots-x y^{n-2}+\right. y^{n-1}$ ) So, $x^n+y^n$ is divisible by $x+y$.

In the given statement :

  • $25^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by $25+3=28$.

  • $25^{13}+3^{13}$ is also divisible by 7 because 28 is divisible by 7 .

  • So, $25^{13}+3^{13}=7 \cdot I_1$.........(1) ( $I_1$ is a positive integer).

Similarly :

  • $20^{13}+8^{13}$ is divisible by $20+8=28$.

  • $20^{13}+8^{13}$ is divisible by 7.

  • So, $20^{13}+8^{13}=7 \cdot I_2$ ..........(2) ( $I_2$ is a positive integer).

Add (1) & (2) :

  • $25^{13}+3^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}=7\left(I_1+I_2\right)$

So, $25^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by 7 . Statement I is true.

Statement : The integral part of $(7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}$ is an odd number.

Let $X=(7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}$. Let $I$ be the integral part and $f$ be the fractional part ( $0

Adding the two expansions:

$$ (7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}+(7-4 \sqrt{3})^{25}=I+f+f^{\prime} $$

Using the binomial expansion $(a+b)^n+(a-b)^n=2\left[a^n+\binom{n}{2} a^{n-2} b^2+\ldots\right]$, we see that the sum is always an even integer because of the factor of 2 .

$$ I+f+f^{\prime}=\text { Even Integer } $$

Since $I$ is an integer, $f+f^{\prime}$ must also be an integer. Given $0 < f < 1$ and $0 < f^{\prime} < 1$, the only possible integer value for their sum is $f+f^{\prime}=1$.

$$ I+1=\text { Even Integer } $$

$$ I=\text { Even Integer }-1=\text { Odd Integer } $$

The integral part I is indeed an odd number. Statement II is True.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
questions.question: The sum of the coefficients of $x^{499}$ and $x^{500}$ in $(1 + x)^{1000} + x(1 + x)^{999} + x^2(1 + x)^{998} + \ldots + x^{1000}$ is :
questions.solution:
Let

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000}+x(1+x)^{999}+x^2(1+x)^{998}+\cdots+x^{1000} $$

Notice that this is a Geometric Progression (GP) with:

  • First term (a): $(1+x)^{1000}$

  • Common ratio $(r): \frac{x}{1+x}$

  • Number of terms ( $n$ ): 1001

The sum of a GP is given by $S=a \frac{\left(1-r^n\right)}{(1-r)}$. Substituting our values:

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000}\left[\frac{1-\left(\frac{x}{1+x}\right)^{1001}}{1-\frac{x}{1+x}}\right] $$

Simplify the denominator: $1-\frac{x}{1+x}=\frac{1+x-x}{1+x}=\frac{1}{1+x}$.

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000} \cdot(1+x)\left[1-\frac{x^{1001}}{(1+x)^{1001}}\right] $$

$$ S=(1+x)^{1001}-x^{1001} $$

Now we need the coefficients of $x^{499}$ and $x^{500}$ in the simplified expression $(1+x)^{1001}- x^{1001}$.

Since $x^{1001}$ only affects the term with power 1001, we only look at $(1+x)^{1001}$.

In the binomial expansion $(1+x)^n$

Coefficient of $x^r$ is ${ }^n C_r$

Coefficient of $x^{499}$ in $S$ :

$$ { }^{1001} C_{499} $$

Coefficient of $x^{500}$ in $S$ :

${ }^{1001} C_{500}$ (The term $x^{1001}$ does not contribute.)

$$ \text { Sum of coefficients } $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} { }^{1001} C_{499}+{ }^{1001} C_{500}={ }^{1002} C_{500} \end{array} $$

[$\text { Using pascal's identity } $

${ }^n C_r+{ }^n C_{r+1}={ }^{n+1} C_{r+1}$]

questions.question: In the expansion of $\left(9 x-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{x}}\right)^{18}, x>0$, if the term independent of $x$ is (221)k, then k is equal to:
questions.solution:
For the expansion of

$$ \left(9x-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^{18}, $$

we use the general term in the binomial expansion:

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{18}{r}(9x)^{18-r}\left(-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^r $$

Now simplify the power of $x$ in this term.

Since

$$ (9x)^{18-r}=9^{18-r}x^{18-r} $$

and

$$ \left(\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^r=\frac{1}{3^r x^{r/2}}, $$

so the power of $x$ in the general term is

$$ x^{18-r-\frac r2}=x^{18-\frac{3r}{2}}. $$

For the term independent of $x$, the power of $x$ must be zero:

$$ 18-\frac{3r}{2}=0 $$

$$ \frac{3r}{2}=18 $$

$$ r=12 $$

So the required term is the $(12+1)$th term, i.e. $T_{13}$.

Now compute it:

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12}(9x)^6\left(-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^{12} $$

Since the power of $x$ becomes zero, only the numerical coefficient remains:

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12}\cdot 9^6 \cdot \frac{1}{3^{12}} $$

Also,

$$ 9^6=(3^2)^6=3^{12} $$

So,

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12} $$

and

$$ \binom{18}{12}=\binom{18}{6}=\frac{18!}{6!\,12!}=18564 $$

Given that the term independent of $x$ is $(221)k$, we have

$$ 221k=18564 $$

Thus,

$$ k=\frac{18564}{221}=84 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{84} $$

Hence, Option A is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: Let the smallest value of $k \in \mathbb{N}$, for which the coefficient of $x^3$ in $(1+x)^3+(1+x)^4+(1+x)^5+\ldots+(1+x)^{99}+(1+k x)^{100}, x \neq 0$, is $\left(43 n+\frac{101}{4}\right)\left({ }^{100} \mathrm{C}_3\right)$ for some $n \in \mathrm{~N}$, be $p$. Then the value of $p+n$ is :
questions.solution:
Coefficient of $x^3$ in the given expression

$$ (1+x)^3+(1+x)^4+\cdots +(1+x)^{99}+(1+kx)^{100} $$

is obtained by adding the coefficients of $x^3$ from each term.

For $(1+x)^r$, coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ {}^rC_3 $$

So, from

$$ (1+x)^3+(1+x)^4+\cdots +(1+x)^{99} $$

the total coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ \sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^rC_3 $$

Also, in $(1+kx)^{100}$, coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ {}^{100}C_3\,k^3 $$

Hence total coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ \sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^rC_3 + {}^{100}C_3 k^3 $$

Now use the standard NCERT identity:

$$ \sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^rC_3 = {}^{100}C_4 $$

So coefficient becomes

$$ {}^{100}C_4 + {}^{100}C_3 k^3 $$

Given that this equals

$$ \left(43n+\frac{101}{4}\right){}^{100}C_3 $$

Therefore,

$$ {}^{100}C_4 + {}^{100}C_3 k^3 = \left(43n+\frac{101}{4}\right){}^{100}C_3 $$

Divide by ${}^{100}C_3$:

$$ \frac{{}^{100}C_4}{{}^{100}C_3}+k^3 = 43n+\frac{101}{4} $$

Now,

$$ \frac{{}^{100}C_4}{{}^{100}C_3} = \frac{100-3}{4} = \frac{97}{4} $$

So,

$$ k^3+\frac{97}{4}=43n+\frac{101}{4} $$

Thus,

$$ k^3=43n+1 $$

We need the smallest $k\in\mathbb N$ such that

$$ k^3-1 $$

is divisible by $43$.

That is,

$$ k^3\equiv 1 \pmod{43} $$

Now check smallest natural numbers:

$$ 1^3=1 \equiv 1 \pmod{43} $$

So the smallest value is

$$ k=1 $$

Hence,

$$ 1=43n+1 $$

which gives

$$ n=0 $$

But in the question, $n\in \mathbb N$. In school-level convention, if $\mathbb N=\{0,1,2,\dots\}$, then this is allowed.

Thus,

$$ p=1,\quad n=0 $$

and

$$ p+n=1 $$

But this is not among the options, so clearly the intended convention is $n\ge 1$.

Now take the smallest $n\in\mathbb N$ with $n\ge 1$ such that

$$ k^3=43n+1 $$

Try $n=1$:

$$ k^3=44 \quad \text{not a cube} $$

$n=2$:

$$ k^3=87 \quad \text{not a cube} $$

$n=3$:

$$ k^3=130 \quad \text{not a cube} $$

$n=4$:

$$ k^3=173 $$

$n=5$:

$$ k^3=216=6^3 $$

So smallest such $k$ is

$$ p=6,\quad n=5 $$

Therefore,

$$ p+n=6+5=11 $$

Hence the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{11} $$

So, Option B is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
questions.question: The coefficient of $x^2$ in the expansion of $\left(2 x^2+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{10}, x \neq 0$, is :
questions.solution:
Using the binomial theorem,

$$ \left(2x^2+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{10} = \sum_{r=0}^{10} \binom{10}{r} (2x^2)^{10-r}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^r $$

Now simplify the general term:

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{10}{r} 2^{10-r} x^{2(10-r)} x^{-r} $$

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{10}{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-2r-r} =\binom{10}{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-3r} $$

We need the coefficient of $x^2$, so set the power of $x$ equal to $2$:

$$ 20-3r=2 $$

$$ 3r=18 $$

$$ r=6 $$

Now substitute $r=6$:

$$ \text{Required coefficient}=\binom{10}{6}2^{10-6} $$

$$ =\binom{10}{6}2^4 $$

$$ =210 \times 16 $$

$$ =3360 $$

So, the coefficient of $x^2$ is

$$ \boxed{3360} $$

Hence, the correct option is B.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
questions.question: If the sum of the coefficients of $x^7$ and $x^{14}$ in the expansion of $\left(\frac{1}{x^3}-x^4\right)^n, x \neq 0$, is zero, then the value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
For

$$ \left(\frac{1}{x^3}-x^4\right)^n = \left(x^{-3}-x^4\right)^n, $$

let us write the general term in the expansion.

The general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{n}{r}(x^{-3})^{\,n-r}(-x^4)^r $$

$$ =\binom{n}{r}(-1)^r x^{-3(n-r)}x^{4r} $$

$$ =\binom{n}{r}(-1)^r x^{-3n+3r+4r} $$

$$ =\binom{n}{r}(-1)^r x^{-3n+7r}. $$

So, the coefficient of $x^k$ comes from the term for which

$$ -3n+7r=k. $$

Now find the coefficients of $x^7$ and $x^{14}$.

For $x^7$:

$$ -3n+7r=7 $$

$$ 7r=3n+7 $$

$$ r=\frac{3n+7}{7}. $$

For $x^{14}$:

$$ -3n+7r=14 $$

$$ 7r=3n+14 $$

$$ r=\frac{3n+14}{7}. $$

Since $r$ must be an integer, $3n+7$ and $3n+14$ must both be divisible by $7$.

Now,

$$ 3n+7\equiv 0 \pmod{7} \Rightarrow 3n\equiv 0 \pmod{7}. $$

Since $3$ and $7$ are coprime,

$$ n\equiv 0 \pmod{7}. $$

So let

$$ n=7m. $$

Then the two corresponding values of $r$ are

For $x^7$:

$$ r_1=\frac{3(7m)+7}{7}=3m+1. $$

For $x^{14}$:

$$ r_2=\frac{3(7m)+14}{7}=3m+2. $$

Hence the coefficients are

$$ \binom{n}{3m+1}(-1)^{3m+1} \quad \text{and} \quad \binom{n}{3m+2}(-1)^{3m+2}. $$

Their sum is zero, so

$$ \binom{n}{3m+1}(-1)^{3m+1}+\binom{n}{3m+2}(-1)^{3m+2}=0. $$

Taking common sign,

$$ -\binom{n}{3m+1}+\binom{n}{3m+2}=0 $$

or

$$ \binom{n}{3m+2}=\binom{n}{3m+1}. $$

Using the NCERT result:

$$ \binom{n}{r}=\binom{n}{r+1} \Rightarrow r+1=\frac{n}{2} $$

or equivalently,

$$ 2r+1=n. $$

Here,

$$ r=3m+1. $$

So,

$$ 2(3m+1)+1=7m $$

$$ 6m+2+1=7m $$

$$ 6m+3=7m $$

$$ m=3. $$

Therefore,

$$ n=7m=21. $$

Hence, the value of $n$ is

$$ \boxed{21}. $$

questions.question: If the coefficients of the middle terms in the binomial expansions of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ and $(1-\alpha x)^{28}, \alpha \neq 0$, are equal, then the value of $\alpha$ is:
questions.solution:
For a binomial expansion $(a+b)^n$:

  • if $n$ is even, there is only one middle term,

  • and the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}+1\right)$th term.

We are given:

$$ (1+\alpha x)^{26} \quad \text{and} \quad (1-\alpha x)^{28} $$

and the coefficients of their middle terms are equal.

1. Middle term of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$

Since $26$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{26}{2}+1\right)=14$th term.

The general term in $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{26}{r}(1)^{26-r}(\alpha x)^r $$

So the $14$th term corresponds to $r=13$:

$$ T_{14}=\binom{26}{13}(\alpha x)^{13} $$

Hence, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13} $$

2. Middle term of $(1-\alpha x)^{28}$

Since $28$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{28}{2}+1\right)=15$th term.

The general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{28}{r}(1)^{28-r}(-\alpha x)^r $$

So the $15$th term corresponds to $r=14$:

$$ T_{15}=\binom{28}{14}(-\alpha x)^{14} $$

Since $14$ is even,

$$ (-\alpha x)^{14}=\alpha^{14}x^{14} $$

Thus, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

3. Equate the coefficients

Given that these coefficients are equal:

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

Since $\alpha\neq 0$, divide both sides by $\alpha^{13}$:

$$ \binom{26}{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha $$

So,

$$ \alpha=\frac{\binom{26}{13}}{\binom{28}{14}} $$

4. Simplify

$$ \alpha=\frac{\dfrac{26!}{13!13!}}{\dfrac{28!}{14!14!}} $$

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!}{13!13!}\cdot \frac{14!14!}{28!} $$

Now use

$$ 14!=14\cdot 13! $$

So,

$$ 14!14!=(14\cdot 13!)(14\cdot 13!)=14^2(13!)^2 $$

Thus,

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!\cdot 14^2(13!)^2}{(13!)^2\cdot 28!} =\frac{26!\cdot 14^2}{28!} $$

Also,

$$ 28!=28\cdot 27\cdot 26! $$

Therefore,

$$ \alpha=\frac{14^2}{28\cdot 27} =\frac{14}{2\cdot 27} =\frac{7}{27} $$

$$ \boxed{\alpha=\frac{7}{27}} $$

So, the correct option is D.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question: If $26\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_2\right)+\frac{2^5}{5}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_4\right)+\frac{2^7}{7}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_6\right)+\cdots+\frac{2^{13}}{13}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_{12}\right)\right)=3^{13}-\alpha$, then $\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}}=26\left[\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left(\frac{2^5}{5}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_4\right)+\left(\frac{2^7}{7}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_6\right)+\left(\frac{2^9}{9}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_8\right)+\ldots .+\left(\frac{2^{13}}{13}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right)\right]$

$=26\left[\sum\limits_{r=1}^6\left(\frac{2^{2 r+1}}{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r}\right)\right]$.

$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}}=\frac{26}{13}\left[\sum\limits_{r=1}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r+1}\right)\right]$

$=2\left[\sum\limits_{r=0}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r+1}\right)-\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right) \cdot 2^1\right]$

$=2\left[\sum\limits_{r=0}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} C_{2 r+1}\right)\right]-52$

$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}} & =2\left[\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right) \cdot 2^1+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_3\right) \cdot 2^3+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_5\right) \cdot 2^5+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_7\right) \cdot 2^7+\ldots . .+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right) \cdot 2^{13}\right]-52 \\ & =(1+2)^{13}-(1-2)^{13}-52 \\ & =3^{13}+1-52 \\ & =3^{13}-51.....(i)\end{aligned}$

$$ \boldsymbol{S}_r=26\left[\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left(\frac{2^5}{5}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_4\right)+\left(\frac{2^7}{7}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_6\right)+\left(\frac{2^9}{9}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_8\right)+\ldots .+\left(\frac{2^{13}}{13}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right)\right]=3^{13}-\boldsymbol{\alpha} \ldots . (ii)$$

From the equivalence relationship between equation $(\boldsymbol{i})$ and $(\boldsymbol{i i})$ we conclude $\alpha=51$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Evening Shift
questions.question:
The correct statements are :

A. Activation energy for enzyme catalysed hydrolysis of sucrose is lower than that of acid catalysed hydrolysis.

B. During denaturation, secondary and tertiary structures of a protein are destroyed but primary structure remains intact.

C. Nucleotides are joined together by glycosidic linkage between $\mathrm{C}_1$ and $\mathrm{C}_4$ carbons of the pentose sugar.

D. Quaternary structure of proteins represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
questions.solution:
A. Activation energy for enzyme catalysed hydrolysis of sucrose is lower than that of acid catalysed hydrolysis.

  • Enzymes are biological catalysts that increase the rate of reactions by lowering their activation energy.

  • Acid catalysis also provides an alternative reaction pathway, but enzymes are typically much more efficient at lowering activation energy under physiological conditions.

  • For the hydrolysis of sucrose, the enzyme sucrase (invertase) significantly reduces the activation energy compared to acid-catalyzed hydrolysis, making the reaction much faster.

  • Therefore, statement A is correct.

B. During denaturation, secondary and tertiary structures of a protein are destroyed but primary structure remains intact.

  • Denaturation is the process by which proteins lose their native three-dimensional structure (secondary, tertiary, and if present, quaternary structures) due to disruptions of weak interactions (like hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds).

  • The primary structure, which is the sequence of amino acids linked by strong covalent peptide bonds, is generally not broken during denaturation. Breaking peptide bonds would require harsh conditions like strong acid/base hydrolysis or specific proteases.

  • Therefore, statement B is correct.

C. Nucleotides are joined together by glycosidic linkage between C1 and C4 carbons of the pentose sugar.

  • Nucleotides are linked together in nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) by phosphodiester bonds.

  • A phosphodiester bond forms between the phosphate group attached to the 5' carbon of one nucleotide and the hydroxyl group on the 3' carbon of the pentose sugar of the next nucleotide.

  • A glycosidic linkage (specifically, an N-glycosidic bond) connects the nitrogenous base to the C1 carbon of the pentose sugar within a single nucleotide. It does not link nucleotides together, nor does it involve C1 and C4 carbons between different pentose sugars.

  • Therefore, statement C is incorrect.

D. Quaternary structure of proteins represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain.

  • The tertiary structure describes the overall three-dimensional folding of a single polypeptide chain.

  • The quaternary structure refers to the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits (each having its own tertiary structure) to form a functional protein complex. It describes how these individual chains associate with each other.

  • Therefore, the statement describes tertiary structure, not quaternary structure.

  • Therefore, statement D is incorrect.

Conclusion:

Statements A and B are correct. Statements C and D are incorrect.

The correct option should include only A and B.

The final answer is $\boxed{\text{A and B Only}}$

questions.question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : The structure of Maltose is given below:

Statement II : The structure of Lactose is given below :

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.solution:
Maltose and lactose are both disaccharides.

In each of these structures, one anomeric carbon is used to form the glycosidic linkage, but the other anomeric carbon remains free as a hemiacetal (–OH at anomeric carbon).

If a disaccharide has a free hemiacetal group, it can open up to the open-chain form and can reduce mild oxidising agents (like Tollens’ reagent or Fehling’s solution).

Therefore, both maltose and lactose are reducing sugars because both contain a hemiacetal linkage (free anomeric carbon).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
Identify the correct statements.

A. Glucose exists in two anomeric forms.

B. Anomers of glucose differ in configuration at $\mathrm{C}-1$ in cyclic hemiacetal structure.

C. Melting point of $\alpha$ - anomer of glucose is greater than $\beta$ - anomer.

D. Specific rotation of $\alpha$ - anomer is $+19^{\circ}$ while for $\beta$ - anomer is $+112^{\circ}$

E. $\alpha$ and $\beta$ - anomers of glucose are prepared by crystallization of saturated glucose solution at 303 K and 371 K respectively.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
A and B Only

B and C Only

A, B and D Only

A, B and E Only
questions.solution:
Statement A: Glucose exists in two anomeric forms.

This statement is correct. Glucose exists in two different crystalline forms, which are named as the $\alpha$-form and the $\beta$-form. These are known as anomers.

Statement B: Anomers of glucose differ in configuration at $\mathrm{C}-1$ in cyclic hemiacetal structure.

This statement is correct. The $\alpha$ and $\beta$ forms of glucose are cyclic hemiacetals that differ only in the spatial arrangement of the hydroxyl group (-OH) at the first carbon atom ($\mathrm{C}-1$). This specific carbon is referred to as the anomeric carbon.

Statement C: Melting point of $\alpha$ - anomer of glucose is greater than $\beta$ - anomer.

This statement is incorrect. According to experimental data, the melting point of the $\alpha$-form of glucose is $419 \text{ K}$, whereas the melting point of the $\beta$-form is slightly higher, at $423 \text{ K}$. Therefore, the melting point of the $\beta$-anomer is greater than that of the $\alpha$-anomer.

Statement D: Specific rotation of $\alpha$ - anomer is $+19^{\circ}$ while for $\beta$ - anomer is $+112^{\circ}$

This statement is incorrect because the values are interchanged. The specific rotation of the pure $\alpha$-anomer is $+112.2^{\circ}$, and the specific rotation of the pure $\beta$-anomer is $+19.2^{\circ}$. Over time, both undergo mutarotation to reach an equilibrium specific rotation of $+52.5^{\circ}$.

Statement E: $\alpha$ and $\beta$ - anomers of glucose are prepared by crystallization of saturated glucose solution at 303 K and 371 K respectively.

This statement is correct. The $\alpha$-form is typically obtained by the crystallization from a concentrated aqueous solution of glucose at room temperature, around $303 \text{ K}$. On the other hand, the $\beta$-form is obtained by crystallizing it from a hot and saturated aqueous solution at $371 \text{ K}$.

From the analysis above, the mathematically and factually correct statements are A, B, and E.

Therefore, the correct choice is Option D.
