questions.question: If $f(x)$ satisfies the relation $f(x)=e^x+\int_0^1\left(y+x e^x\right) f(y) d y$, then $e+f(0)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=e^x+\int_0^1\left(y+x e^x\right) f(y) d y \\ & f(x)=e^x+\int_0^1 y f(y) d y+\int_0^1 x e^x f(y) d y \end{aligned} $$

Integration is w.r.t $y$, so variable $x$ is taken as a constant.

$$ f(x)=e^x+\int_0^1 y f(y) d y+x e^x \int_0^1 f(y) d y . $$

Let $A=\int_0^1 y f(y) d y$ and $B=\int_0^1 f(y) d y$, both are constants.

So $f(x)$ becomes,

$$ f(x)=e^x+A+B x e^x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(1)$$

Or, $f(y)=e^y+A+B y e^y\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(2)$

Solve, $B=\int_0^1 f(y) d y$.

$$ B=\int_0^1\left[e^y+A+B y e^y\right] d y,\left\{\text { from (2) } f(y)=e^y+A+B y e^y\right\} . $$

$$ B=\int_0^1\left[e^y+A+B y e^y\right] d y \text {, \{from (2) } f(y)=e^y+A+B y e^y \text { \}. } $$

Using integration by parts, $\int y e^y d y=y e^y-\int e^y=e^y(y-1)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & B=\left[e^y+A y+B e^y(y-1)\right]_0^1 . \\ & B=\left[e^1+A \cdot 1+0-e^0-0-B \cdot e^0(-1)\right] . \\ & B=e+A-1+B \Longrightarrow A=1-e . \end{aligned} $$

Finding $f(0)$ using equation (1).

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(0)=1+A+0 . \\ & f(0)=1+1-e,\{\text { substitute } A=1-e\} . \\ & f(0)+e=2\end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift
questions.question: Let a differentiable function $f$ satisfy the equation $\int_0^{36} f\left(\frac{t x}{36}\right) d t=4 \alpha f(x)$. If $y=f(x)$ is a standard parabola passing through the points $(2,1)$ and $(-4, \beta)$, then $\beta^\alpha$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
Given, $\int_0^{36} f\left(\frac{t x}{36}\right) d t=4 \alpha f(x)$

Let $\frac{t x}{36}=u \Rightarrow \frac{x}{36} d t=d u$

$$ \int_0^x f(u) \frac{36}{x} d u=4 \alpha f(x) $$

Integration is w.r.t $u$, so treat $x$ as a constant

$$ \int_0^x f(u) d u=\frac{4 x}{36} \alpha f(x)=\frac{x}{9} \alpha f(x) $$

Now, differentiate both sides w.r.t $x$, use Leibniz theorem in LHS:

$$ 1 \cdot f(x)=\frac{\alpha}{9}\left[1 \cdot f(x)+x f^{\prime}(x)\right] $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $9 f(x)=\alpha f(x)+\alpha x f^{\prime}(x)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\alpha x f^{\prime}(x)=f(x)(9-\alpha)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)}=\frac{9-\alpha}{\alpha x}$

Integrating both sides

$$ \int \frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)} d x=\int \frac{9-\alpha}{\alpha}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\ln f(x)=\frac{9-\alpha}{\alpha} \ln x+c$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $f(x)=e^{\frac{9-\alpha}{\alpha} \ln x} \cdot e^c$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ f(x)=x^{\frac{9-\alpha}{\alpha}} \cdot K \quad\left\{K=e^c\right\} $$

It is given that $f(x)$ is a standard parabola passing through $(2,1)$ and $(-4, \beta)$.

So $f(x)=K x^2$ type.

$\frac{9-\alpha}{\alpha}=2 \Longrightarrow 9-\alpha=2 \alpha \Longrightarrow 3 \alpha=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \alpha=3 $$

$f(x)=K x^2$ passing through $(2,1)$

$$ f(2)=1 $$

$$ 1=K(2)^2 \Longrightarrow K=\frac{1}{4} $$

$$ f(x)=\frac{1}{4} x^2 $$

$f(x)$ is also passing through $(-4, \beta), f(-4)=\beta$

$$ \beta=\frac{1}{4}(-4)^2=\frac{16}{4}=4 $$

then $\beta^\alpha=4^3=64$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let [.] denote the greatest integer function. Then

$$ \int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \left( \frac{12(3+[x])}{3+\left[\sin x\right]+\left[\cos x\right]} \right) dx $$
is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$ I=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{12(3+[x])}{3+[\sin x]+[\cos x]}\right) d x $$

Breakdown the Interval Since $\frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.57$ and $-\frac{\pi}{2} \approx-1.57$, we break the integral at the integer points $x=-1,0,1$.

Interval [x] [sin x] [cos x] Integrand f(x)
$\left[-\frac{\pi}{2},-1\right)$ −2 −1 0 $\frac{12(3-2)}{3-1+0}=\frac{12}{2}=6$
[−1, 0) −1 −1 0 $\frac{12(3-1)}{3-1+0}=\frac{24}{2}=12$
[0, 1) 0 0 0 $\frac{12(3+0)}{3+0+0}=\frac{36}{3}=12$
$\left[1, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ 1 0 0 $\frac{12(3+1)}{3+0+0}=\frac{48}{3}=16$

Notes on values :

  • 1. For $x \in(0,1), 0<\cos x<1$, so $[\cos x]=0$. (At $x=0,[\cos x]=1$, but a single point doesn't change the integral).

    2. For $x \in\left[1, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), 0<\sin x<1$, so $[\sin x]=0$.

To find the total value, we integrate the constant values found above over their respective subintervals:

$$ I=\int\limits_{-\pi / 2}^{-1} 6 d x+\int\limits_{-1}^1 12 d x+\int\limits_1^{\pi / 2} 16 d x $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ I=6[x]_{-\pi / 2}^{-1}+12[x]_{-1}^1+16[x]_1^{\pi / 2} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ I=6\left(-1-\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right)+12(1-(-1))+16\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ I=-6+3 \pi+24+8 \pi-16 \\ I=11 \pi+2 $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let $f$ be a differentiable function satisfying $f(x) = 1 - 2x + \int\limits_0^x e^{(x-t)} f(t)\,dt$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and let

$g(x) = \int\limits_0^x (f(t) + 2)^{15} (t - 4)^6 (t + 12)^{17}\,dt$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

If $p$ and $q$ are respectively the points of local minima and local maxima of $g$, then the value of $|p+q|$ is equal to ________.
questions.solution:
The given equation is:

$$ f(x)=1-2 x+\int_0^x e^{(x-t)} f(t) d t $$

Since $e^{(x-t)}=e^x \cdot e^{-t}$, we can move $e^x$ outside the integral because it is constant relative to $t$

$$ f(x)=1-2 x+e^x \int_0^x e^{-t} f(t) d t $$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ using the Leibniz Rule and the product rule:

$$ f^{\prime}(x)=-2+\left[e^x \int_0^x e^{-t} f(t) d t+e^x\left(e^{-x} f(x)\right)\right] $$

Now, we use substitution from our original equation. Notice that $e^x \int_0^x e^{-t} f(t) d t=f(x)- (1-2 x)$. Substituting this back into the derivative expression:

$$ f^{\prime}(x)=-2+[f(x)-1+2 x]+f(x) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ f^{\prime}(x)=2 f(x)+2 x-3 $$

Combine the terms to get the linear differential equatio in the form of $\frac{d f(x)}{d x}+P(x) f(x)=Q(x)$

$$ \frac{d f(x)}{d x}-2 f(x)=2 x-3 $$

Solve for $f(x)$

Integrating Factor (IF): $e^{\int-2 d x}=e^{-2 x}$

General Solution: $f(x) \cdot e^{-2 x}=\int(2 x-3) e^{-2 x} d x$

By integrating the right side (Integration by Parts), we get $f(x)=1-x+C e^{2 x}$.

Using $f(0)=1$ (from the original equation), we find $C=0$.

Result: $f(x)=1-x$.

Find the Extrema of $g(x)$

$$ g(x)=\int_0^x(f(t)+2)^{15}(t-4)^6(t+12)^{17} d t $$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ using the Leibniz Rule

$$ g^{\prime}(x)=(f(x)+2)^{15}(x-4)^6(x+12)^{17} $$

Substitute $f(x)=1-x$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} g^{\prime}(x)=(1-x+2)^{15}(x-4)^6(x+12)^{17} \\ g^{\prime}(x)=(3-x)^{15}(x-4)^6(x+12)^{17} \end{gathered} $$

Critical points: $x=3,4,-12$.

Plot on number line to find local maxima and minima.

Local Minima ( $p$ ): At $x=-12, g^{\prime}(x)$ changes sign from negative to positive. Thus, $p=-12$.

Local Maxima ( $q$ ): At $x=3, g^{\prime}(x)$ changes sign from positive to negative. Thus, $q=3$.

$x=4$ : No sign change occurs (even power 6), so it is not a local extremum.

Final Answer

$$ |p+q|=|-12+3|=|-9|=9 $$

The value of $|p+q|$ is 9 .

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
questions.question:
Let $f$ be a polynomial function such that $f\left(x^2+1\right)=x^4+5 x^2+2$, for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

Then $\int\limits_0^3 f(x) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We are given the functional equation:

$$ f\left(x^2+1\right)=x^4+5 x^2+2 $$

Let $t=x^2+1$. This implies that $x^2=t-1$. Now, substitute this into the right side of the equation:

$$ \begin{gathered} f(t)=(t-1)^2+5(t-1)+2 \\ f(t)=\left(t^2-2 t+1\right)+5 t-5+2 \\ f(t)=t^2+3 t-2 \end{gathered} $$

Replacing $t$ back with $x$, the polynomial function is:

$$ f(x)=x^2+3 x-2 $$

Now, we calculate the definite integral from 0 to 3 :

$$ \begin{gathered} \quad \int_0^3 f(x) d x=\int_0^3\left(x^2+3 x-2\right) d x \\ {\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+\frac{3 x^2}{2}-2 x\right]_0^3} \\ =\left(\frac{3^3}{3}+\frac{3\left(3^2\right)}{2}-2(3)\right)-(0) \\ =\left(\frac{27}{3}+\frac{27}{2}-6\right)=9+\frac{27}{2}-6=3+\frac{27}{2} \\ =\frac{6+27}{2}=\frac{33}{2} \end{gathered} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
questions.question: $$ \text { The value of } \sum\limits_{r=1}^{20}\left(\left|\sqrt{\pi\left(\int\limits_0^r x|\sin \pi x| d x\right)}\right|\right) \text { is } $$
questions.solution:
Let $J_r=\int\limits_0^r x \cdot|\sin \pi x| d x$

So we need to calculate

$$ I=\sum\limits_{r=1}^{20} \sqrt{\pi J_r} $$

So,

$$ I=\sqrt{\pi}\left(J_1+J_2+J_3+\cdots+J_{20}\right) $$

Let $\pi x=t \Rightarrow \pi d x=d t$

$$ J_r=\int\limits_0^{\pi r} \frac{t}{\pi}|\sin t| \frac{d t}{\pi}=\frac{1}{\pi^2} \int\limits_0^{\pi r} t|\sin t| d t $$

Period of $|\sin t|$ is $\pi$, so break the interval

$\begin{aligned} & \int\limits_0^{\pi r} t|\sin t|=\int\limits_0^\pi t|\sin t| d t+\int\limits_\pi^{2 \pi} t|\sin t| d t+\int\limits_{2 \pi}^{3 \pi} t|\sin t| d t+\cdots \int\limits_{(r-1) \pi}^{r \pi} t|\sin t| d t \\ & \text { Now } \\ & \quad|\sin t|=\sin t \text { for Ist and II Quadrant } \\ & \quad|\sin t|=-\sin t \text { for III and IV Quadrant }\end{aligned}$

$$ \int\limits_0^{r \pi} t|\sin t| d t=\int\limits_0^\pi t \sin t d t-\int\limits_\pi^{2 \pi} t \sin t d t+\int\limits_{2 \pi}^{3 \pi} t \sin t d t-\int\limits_{3 \pi}^{4 \pi} t \sin t \ldots(-1)^{r-1} \int\limits_{(r-1) \pi}^{r \pi} t \sin t d t $$

Solving $\int t \cdot \sin t$ using by parts

$$ \int u \cdot v d x=u \cdot \int v d x-\int\left(u^{\prime} \int v d v\right) d x $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \int t \cdot \sin t=(-t \cos t)-\int 1 \cdot(-\cos t)=-t \cos t+\sin t $$

Substituting the limits

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\begin{aligned} & \int\limits_0^{\pi r} t \cdot|\sin t| d t=[-t \cos t+\sin t]_0^\pi-[-t \cos t+\sin t]_\pi^{2 \pi}+[-t \cos t+ \sin t]_{2 \pi}^{3 \pi}-[-t \cos t+\sin t]_{3 \pi}^{4 \pi} \cdots \int\limits_{(r-1) \pi}^{r \pi} t \sin t(-1)^{r-1} d t\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =[\pi+0-0]-[-2 \pi+0-\{\pi+0\}]+[3 \pi+0-\{-2 \pi\}+0]-[-4 \pi- \{-3 \pi+0\}]+\cdots \int\limits_{(r-1) \pi}^{r \pi} t \sin t(-1)^{r-1} d t\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\int\limits_0^{r \pi} t|\sin t| d t=[\pi+0]+[2 \pi+\pi]+[3 \pi+2 \pi]+[4 \pi+3 \pi] \ldots[r \pi+(r-1) \pi]$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\pi+3 \pi+5 \pi+7 \pi+\ldots(2 r-1) \pi \\\\ & =\pi(1+3+5+7+\ldots(2 r-1)) \end{aligned} $$

Sum of first $r$ odd numbers is $r^2$

$$ \int\limits_0^{r \pi} t|\sin t| d t=\pi r^2 $$

$$ J_r=\frac{1}{\pi^2} \pi r^2=\frac{r^2}{\pi} $$

so,

$$ I=\sum\limits_{r=1}^{20} \sqrt{\pi \cdot J_r}=\sum\limits_{r=1}^{20} \sqrt{\pi \cdot \frac{r^2}{\pi}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=\sum\limits_{r=1}^{20} r=1+2+3+\ldots 20$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=\frac{20 \times 21}{2}=210$

questions.question: The value of $\int\limits_{0}^{20\pi} (\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x) dx$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
$$I=\int\limits_{0}^{20\pi} \left(1-2\sin^2 x\cos^2 x\right)\cdot dx$$

$$=20\pi-\frac{1}{2}\int\limits_{0}^{20\pi} \sin^2 2x\,dx$$

$$= 20\pi-\frac{1}{2}(40)\int\limits_{0}^{\pi/2} \sin^2 2x$$

$$= 20\pi-\frac{20}{2}\left(x-\frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right)_{0}^{\pi/2}$$

$$= 20\pi-10\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=15\pi$$

questions.question: If $\alpha = \int\limits_{0}^{2\sqrt{3}} \log_{2}(x^{2} + 4) \, dx + \int\limits_{2}^{4} \sqrt{2x - 4} \, dx$, then $\alpha^{2}$ is equal to ________.
questions.solution:
As we know that

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}+\int_{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})}^{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~b})} \mathrm{f}^{-1}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{bf}(\mathrm{~b})-\mathrm{af}(\mathrm{a}) \\ & \therefore \alpha=(2 \sqrt{3})(4)-(0)(2) \\ & =8 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha^2=192 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
questions.question: The integral $\int\limits_0^1 \cot ^{-1}\left(1+x+x^2\right) d x$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Let

$$ I=\int_0^1 \cot^{-1}(1+x+x^2)\,dx $$

We will use the standard identity

$$ \cot^{-1} t=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{t}\right)\quad \text{for } t>0 $$

Since $1+x+x^2>0$ on $[0,1]$, we can write

$$ I=\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1+x+x^2}\right)\,dx $$

Now use the formula

$$ \tan^{-1} a-\tan^{-1} b=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a-b}{1+ab}\right) $$

We try to express

$$ \frac{1}{1+x+x^2} $$

in a suitable form. Observe that

$$ \tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{(x+1)-x}{1+x(x+1)}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1+x+x^2}\right) $$

Hence,

$$ \cot^{-1}(1+x+x^2)=\tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x $$

So,

$$ I=\int_0^1 \left[\tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x\right]dx $$

Therefore,

$$ I=\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(x+1)\,dx-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx $$

In the first integral, put $u=x+1$. Then when $x=0$, $u=1$, and when $x=1$, $u=2$. So

$$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(x+1)\,dx=\int_1^2 \tan^{-1}u\,du $$

Thus,

$$ I=\int_1^2 \tan^{-1}u\,du-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \int_1^2 \tan^{-1}x\,dx-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx $$

Now use

$$ \int \tan^{-1}x\,dx=x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2) $$

So,

$$ \int_1^2 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_1^2 $$

and

$$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_0^1 $$

Hence,

$$ I= \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_1^2 - \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_0^1 $$

This becomes

$$ I= \left(2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5\right) - \left(\tan^{-1}1-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) -\left(\tan^{-1}1-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) $$

since at $x=0$, the value is $0$.

Now $\tan^{-1}1=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, so

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5-2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) $$

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5-\frac{\pi}{2}+\log 2 $$

Now combine the logarithms:

$$ \log 2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5 = \frac{1}{2}\log 4-\frac{1}{2}\log 5 = \frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) $$

Therefore,

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2} $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log_e\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
If $\alpha=1$ and $\beta=1+i \sqrt{2}$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$ are two roots of the equation

$x^3+a x^2+b x+c=0, a, b, \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\int_{-1}^1\left(x^3+a x^2+b x+c\right) d x$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
Since the coefficients $a,b,c$ are real, complex roots occur in conjugate pairs.

Given two roots are:

$$ \alpha=1,\qquad \beta=1+i\sqrt{2} $$

So the third root must be:

$$ 1-i\sqrt{2} $$

Hence the cubic polynomial is:

$$ x^3+ax^2+bx+c=(x-1)\bigl(x-(1+i\sqrt{2})\bigr)\bigl(x-(1-i\sqrt{2})\bigr) $$

Now first multiply the conjugate factors:

$$ \bigl(x-(1+i\sqrt{2})\bigr)\bigl(x-(1-i\sqrt{2})\bigr) $$

This is of the form:

$$ (A-iB)(A+iB)=A^2+B^2 $$

with

$$ A=x-1,\qquad B=\sqrt{2} $$

So,

$$ \bigl(x-(1+i\sqrt{2})\bigr)\bigl(x-(1-i\sqrt{2})\bigr) =(x-1)^2+2 $$

Therefore,

$$ x^3+ax^2+bx+c=(x-1)\bigl((x-1)^2+2\bigr) $$

Let $y=x-1$. Then:

$$ (x-1)\bigl((x-1)^2+2\bigr)=(x-1)^3+2(x-1) $$

Now expand:

$$ (x-1)^3=x^3-3x^2+3x-1 $$

So,

$$ x^3+ax^2+bx+c=x^3-3x^2+3x-1+2x-2 $$

$$ =x^3-3x^2+5x-3 $$

Now evaluate:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(x^3+ax^2+bx+c)\,dx = \int_{-1}^{1}(x^3-3x^2+5x-3)\,dx $$

Using symmetry:

  • $x^3$ is an odd function, so

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}x^3\,dx=0 $$

  • $5x$ is also odd, so

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}5x\,dx=0 $$

Thus,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(x^3-3x^2+5x-3)\,dx = \int_{-1}^{1}(-3x^2-3)\,dx $$

Now,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}x^2\,dx=\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{1}=\frac13-\left(-\frac13\right)=\frac23 $$

Therefore,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(-3x^2)\,dx=-3\cdot \frac23=-2 $$

Also,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(-3)\,dx=-3(2)=-6 $$

So total value:

$$ -2-6=-8 $$

Hence,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}\left(x^3+ax^2+bx+c\right)\,dx=-8 $$

The correct option is:

$$ \boxed{-8} $$

So, Option C is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: Let $f$ be a real polynomial of degree $n$ such that $f(x)=f^{\prime}(x) f^{\prime \prime}(x)$, for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. If $f(0)=0$, then $36\left(f^{\prime}(2)+f^{\prime \prime}(2)+\int_0^2 f(x) d x\right)$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
Let the degree of the real polynomial $f$ be $n$.

Since $f(x)=f^{\prime}(x)f^{\prime\prime}(x)$ for all $x$, we compare degrees:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \deg(f)=\deg(f^{\prime})+\deg(f^{\prime\prime}) \\ & n=(n-1)+(n-2)=2n-3 \\ & \Rightarrow n=3 \end{aligned} $$

So $f$ must be a cubic polynomial. Write

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=a x^3+b x^2+c x+d \end{aligned} $$

Given $f(0)=0$, we get $d=0$. Hence

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=a x^3+b x^2+c x \end{aligned} $$

Now find derivatives:

$$ \begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=3 a x^2+2 b x+c \\ & f^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 a x+2 b \end{aligned} $$

Use the given condition $f(x)=f^{\prime}(x)f^{\prime\prime}(x)$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(a x^3+b x^2+c x\right)=\left(3 a x^2+2 b x+c\right)(6 a x+2 b) \end{aligned} $$

Now compare coefficients of like powers of $x$.

For $x^3$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & a=18 a^2 \\ & \Rightarrow a=\frac{1}{18}\ \ (\text{since }a\neq 0\text{ for a cubic}) \end{aligned} $$

For $x^2$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & b=6 a b+12 a b \\ & b=18ab \end{aligned} $$

With $a=\frac{1}{18}$, this becomes $b=b$, so it gives no new condition on $b$.

For $x$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & c=4 b^2+6 a c \\ & c=4b^2+\frac{c}{3} \quad (\because 6a=\frac{1}{3})\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2c}{3}=4b^2 \Rightarrow c=6b^2 \end{aligned} $$

For the constant term:

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0=2bc \Rightarrow bc=0 \end{aligned} $$

But $c=6b^2$, so $bc=b(6b^2)=6b^3=0 \Rightarrow b=0$, and then $c=0$.

So the polynomial is

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{1}{18}x^3 \end{aligned} $$

Now compute the required value:

$$ \begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{6}x^2 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(2)=\frac{1}{6}\cdot 4=\frac{2}{3} \\ & f^{\prime\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{3}x \Rightarrow f^{\prime\prime}(2)=\frac{1}{3}\cdot 2=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \int_0^2 f(x)\,dx=\int_0^2 \frac{1}{18}x^3\,dx=\frac{1}{18}\left[\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^2=\frac{1}{18}\cdot \frac{16}{4}=\frac{2}{9} \end{aligned} $$

Add them:

$$ \begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(2)+f^{\prime\prime}(2)+\int_0^2 f(x)\,dx \\ &= \frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9}=\frac{14}{9} \end{aligned} $$

Finally,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 36\left(f^{\prime}(2)+f^{\prime\prime}(2)+\int_0^2 f(x)\,dx\right)=36\cdot \frac{14}{9}=56 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: Let $\int\limits_{-2}^2(|\sin x|+[x \sin x]) d x=2(3-\cos 2)+\beta$, where [ ⋅ ] is the greatest integer function. Then $\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right)$ equals:
questions.solution:
Let the given integral be denoted by $I$. We can split $I$ into two separate integrals:

$$I = \int\limits_{-2}^2 (|\sin x| + [x \sin x]) dx = \int\limits_{-2}^2 |\sin x| dx + \int\limits_{-2}^2 [x \sin x] dx$$

Let $I_1 = \int\limits_{-2}^2 |\sin x| dx$ and $I_2 = \int\limits_{-2}^2 [x \sin x] dx$.

Step 1: Evaluate $I_1$

Based on the property of definite integrals, if $f(x)$ is an even function (i.e., $f(-x) = f(x)$), then $\int_{-a}^a f(x) dx = 2 \int_0^a f(x) dx$.

Since $|\sin(-x)| = |-\sin x| = |\sin x|$, the function is even. Thus,

$$I_1 = 2 \int\limits_0^2 |\sin x| dx$$

In the interval $[0, 2]$, $2$ radians is less than $\pi \ (\approx 3.14)$, so $\sin x > 0$. Hence, $|\sin x| = \sin x$.

$$I_1 = 2 \int\limits_0^2 \sin x dx$$

$$I_1 = 2 [-\cos x]_0^2 = 2 (-\cos 2 - (-\cos 0))$$

$$I_1 = 2 (1 - \cos 2) = 2 - 2 \cos 2$$

Step 2: Evaluate $I_2$

Let $f(x) = x \sin x$. We check if it is even or odd:

$f(-x) = (-x) \sin(-x) = (-x) (-\sin x) = x \sin x = f(x)$.

Since $f(x)$ is an even function, $[x \sin x]$ is also an even function.

$$I_2 = 2 \int\limits_0^2 [x \sin x] dx$$

Now, let us analyze the behavior of $f(x) = x \sin x$ in the interval $x \in [0, 2]$:

  • When $x = 0$, $f(0) = 0$.

  • When $x = 2$, $f(2) = 2 \sin 2$.

Notice that $\pi/2 < 2 < 2\pi/3$, meaning $\sin 2$ is between $\sin(\pi/2)=1$ and $\sin(2\pi/3)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$.

So, $1.732 < 2 \sin 2 < 2$. Hence, the maximum value of $f(x)$ at $x=2$ is between $1$ and $2$.

Let's verify if $f(x)$ is strictly increasing in $[0, 2]$. We differentiate $f(x)$ with respect to $x$:

$$f'(x) = \sin x + x \cos x$$

For $x \in (0, \pi/2]$, both $\sin x > 0$ and $\cos x \ge 0$, so $f'(x) > 0$.

For $x \in (\pi/2, 2]$, $\cos x < 0$. We can rewrite $f'(x)$ as:

$$f'(x) = \cos x (\tan x + x)$$

Since $\tan x$ is negative in the second quadrant and $2$ radians is smaller than $\tan^{-1}(-2)$, the expression $(\tan x + x)$ evaluates to a negative value. Therefore, $\cos x$ (negative) $\times$ $(\tan x + x)$ (negative) will yield $f'(x) > 0$.

Since $f(x)$ strictly increases from $0$ to a value between $1$ and $2$, there is exactly one real number $\alpha \in (0, 2)$ at which $f(\alpha) = 1$. So,

$$\alpha \sin \alpha = 1$$

For $x \in [0, \alpha)$, the function values are $0 \le x \sin x < 1$, which gives $[x \sin x] = 0$.

For $x \in [\alpha, 2]$, the function values are $1 \le x \sin x < 2$, which gives $[x \sin x] = 1$.

Now, split the integral $I_2$ at $x = \alpha$:

$$I_2 = 2 \left( \int\limits_0^\alpha 0 dx + \int\limits_\alpha^2 1 dx \right)$$

$$I_2 = 2 [x]_\alpha^2 = 2(2 - \alpha) = 4 - 2\alpha$$

Step 3: Finding the value of $\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right)$

Combining $I_1$ and $I_2$, we get the total integral:

$$I = I_1 + I_2 = (2 - 2 \cos 2) + (4 - 2\alpha) = 6 - 2 \cos 2 - 2\alpha$$

$$I = 2(3 - \cos 2) - 2\alpha$$

According to the question, the total integral evaluates to:

$$I = 2(3 - \cos 2) + \beta$$

Comparing our result with the given equation, we find:

$$\beta = -2\alpha$$

Finally, substituting $\beta = -2\alpha$ into the expression $\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right)$:

$$\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right) = -2\alpha \cdot \sin\left(\frac{-2\alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right) = -2\alpha \cdot \sin(-\alpha) = -2\alpha (-\sin \alpha)$$

$$\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right) = 2 (\alpha \sin \alpha)$$

From our earlier deduction in Step 2, we established that $\alpha \sin \alpha = 1$. Therefore:

$$\beta \sin \left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right) = 2 (1) = 2$$

Option B is the correct answer.

questions.question: Let $\left(2^{1-\mathrm{a}}+2^{1+\mathrm{a}}\right), f(\mathrm{a}),\left(3^{\mathrm{a}}+3^{-\mathrm{a}}\right)$ be in A.P. and $\alpha$ be the minimum value of $f(\mathrm{a})$. Then the value of the integral $\int\limits_{\log _e(\alpha-1)}^{\log _e(\alpha)} \frac{d x}{\left(e^{2 x}-e^{-2 x}\right)}$ is :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & f(a)=\frac{1}{2}\left(2\left(2^a+2^{-a}\right)+\left(3^a+3^{-a}\right)\right) \\ & a=\frac{1}{2}(2 \times 2+2)=3 \\ & I=\int_{\ln (3-1)}^{\ln 3} \frac{e^{2 x}}{e^{4 x}-1} d x \\ & \text { put } e^{2 x}=t \\ & I=\frac{1}{2} \int_4^9 \frac{d t}{t^2-1} \\ & I=\frac{1}{4}\left(\ln \left(\frac{t-1}{t+1}\right)\right)_4^9 \\ & I=\frac{1}{4} \ln \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.question: The value of the integral $\int\limits_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}}\left(\frac{4-\operatorname{cosec}^2 x}{\cos ^4 x}\right) d x$ is :
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Put } x=2 t \Rightarrow d x=2 d t \\ & I=\int_0^{\infty} \frac{\ell n 2 t}{4 t^2+4}(2 d t)=\frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\ell n 2+\ell n t}{t^2+1} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\ell n 2}{t^2+1} d t+\frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\ell n t}{t^2+1} d t\end{aligned}$

= $\left[\frac{\ln 2}{2} \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_0^{\infty}+\mathrm{I}_1$

$=\frac{\ell \mathrm{n} 2}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}+\mathrm{I}_1$

$\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\ell \mathrm{nt}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \mathrm{dt}$

$t=\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow d t=-\frac{d u}{u^2}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{\infty}^0 \frac{\ell \mathrm{n}(1 / \mathrm{u})}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}^2}+1}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{u}^2}\right) \\ & \operatorname{Add} \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_1=0 \\ & \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi \ell \mathrm{n} 2}{4}\end{aligned}$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
questions.question: The value of the integral $\int\limits_0^{\infty} \frac{\log _e(x)}{x^2+4} d x$ is:
questions.solution:
Let

$$ I=\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln x}{x^2+4}\,dx $$

We will use a standard substitution, as done in NCERT-type definite integral problems.

Take

$$ x=2t $$

Then

$$ dx=2\,dt $$

and when $x=0,\ t=0$, and when $x\to\infty,\ t\to\infty$.

So,

$$ I=\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln(2t)}{(2t)^2+4}\cdot 2\,dt $$

Simplify the denominator:

$$ (2t)^2+4=4t^2+4=4(1+t^2) $$

Hence,

$$ I=\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln(2t)}{4(1+t^2)}\cdot 2\,dt =\frac12\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln(2t)}{1+t^2}\,dt $$

Now use

$$ \ln(2t)=\ln 2+\ln t $$

So,

$$ I=\frac12\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln 2}{1+t^2}\,dt+\frac12\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln t}{1+t^2}\,dt $$

That is,

$$ I=\frac{\ln 2}{2}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{dt}{1+t^2}+\frac12\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln t}{1+t^2}\,dt $$

Now,

$$ \int_0^{\infty}\frac{dt}{1+t^2}=\left[\tan^{-1} t\right]_0^{\infty}=\frac{\pi}{2} $$

Also, let

$$ J=\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\ln t}{1+t^2}\,dt $$

Using the substitution $t=\frac1u$, we get

$$ dt=-\frac{1}{u^2}\,du $$

Then

$$ J=\int_{\infty}^{0}\frac{\ln(1/u)}{1+1/u^2}\left(-\frac{1}{u^2}\right)du $$

This simplifies to

$$ J=\int_0^{\infty}\frac{-\ln u}{1+u^2}\,du =-J $$

Therefore,

$$ J=0 $$

So,

$$ I=\frac{\ln 2}{2}\cdot \frac{\pi}{2} =\frac{\pi\ln 2}{4} $$

Hence, the value of the integral is

$$ \boxed{\frac{\pi\ln 2}{4}} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option B}} $$

questions.question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & \alpha \\ 0 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ be a singular matrix. Let $f(x)=\int_0^x\left(\mathrm{t}^2+2 \mathrm{t}+3\right) \mathrm{dt}, x \in[1, \alpha]$. If M and m are respectively the maximum and the minimum values of $f$ in $[1, \alpha]$, then $3(\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{m})$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Since $A$ is a singular matrix, its determinant must be zero.

We have

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & -1\\ 2 & 1 & \alpha\\ 0 & 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$

So,

$$ |A|=0 $$

Now let us calculate the determinant by expanding along the first row:

$$ |A|=1\begin{vmatrix}1 & \alpha\\ 1 & -1\end{vmatrix} -3\begin{vmatrix}2 & \alpha\\ 0 & -1\end{vmatrix} +(-1)\begin{vmatrix}2 & 1\\ 0 & 1\end{vmatrix} $$

$$ =1[(1)(-1)-(\alpha)(1)]-3[(2)(-1)-(\alpha)(0)]-1[(2)(1)-(1)(0)] $$

$$ =(-1-\alpha)-3(-2)-2 $$

$$ =-1-\alpha+6-2 $$

$$ =3-\alpha $$

Since $|A|=0$,

$$ 3-\alpha=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=3 $$

Now,

$$ f(x)=\int_0^x (t^2+2t+3)\,dt $$

Integrating,

$$ f(x)=\left[\frac{t^3}{3}+t^2+3t\right]_0^x =\frac{x^3}{3}+x^2+3x $$

We need maximum and minimum values of $f(x)$ in $[1,\alpha]=[1,3]$.

Now check whether $f(x)$ is increasing or decreasing.

$$ f'(x)=x^2+2x+3 $$

$$ f'(x)=x^2+2x+3=(x+1)^2+2>0 \quad \text{for all } x $$

So $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $[1,3]$.

Hence,

  • minimum value at $x=1$

  • maximum value at $x=3$

Now,

$$ m=f(1)=\frac{1}{3}+1+3=\frac{13}{3} $$

and

$$ M=f(3)=\frac{27}{3}+9+9=9+9+9=27 $$

Therefore,

$$ M-m=27-\frac{13}{3} =\frac{81-13}{3} =\frac{68}{3} $$

So,

$$ 3(M-m)=3\cdot \frac{68}{3}=68 $$

Hence the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{68} $$

So, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{Option B}}$.

questions.question: The value of the integral $\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(\frac{32 \cos ^4 x}{1+e^{\sin x}}\right) d x$ is :
questions.solution:
Let

$$ I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{32\cos^4 x}{1+e^{\sin x}}\,dx $$

We will use the standard property:

If

$$ I=\int_{-a}^{a} f(x)\,dx, $$

then

$$ I=\int_{-a}^{a} f(-x)\,dx $$

and hence

$$ 2I=\int_{-a}^{a} \bigl(f(x)+f(-x)\bigr)\,dx $$

Now here,

$$ f(x)=\frac{32\cos^4 x}{1+e^{\sin x}} $$

So,

$$ f(-x)=\frac{32\cos^4(-x)}{1+e^{\sin(-x)}} $$

Using $\cos(-x)=\cos x$ and $\sin(-x)=-\sin x$,

$$ f(-x)=\frac{32\cos^4 x}{1+e^{-\sin x}} $$

Therefore,

$$ f(x)+f(-x) = 32\cos^4 x\left(\frac{1}{1+e^{\sin x}}+\frac{1}{1+e^{-\sin x}}\right) $$

Now simplify the bracket:

$$ \frac{1}{1+e^{\sin x}}+\frac{1}{1+e^{-\sin x}} $$

Let $t=e^{\sin x}$. Then $e^{-\sin x}=\frac{1}{t}$. So,

$$ \frac{1}{1+t}+\frac{1}{1+\frac{1}{t}} = \frac{1}{1+t}+\frac{t}{1+t} = 1 $$

Hence,

$$ f(x)+f(-x)=32\cos^4 x $$

So,

$$ 2I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} 32\cos^4 x\,dx $$

Thus,

$$ I=16\int_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \cos^4 x\,dx $$

Since $\cos^4 x$ is an even function,

$$ I=32\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \cos^4 x\,dx $$

Now use the identity

$$ \cos^4 x=\left(\cos^2 x\right)^2 =\left(\frac{1+\cos 2x}{2}\right)^2 $$

which gives

$$ \cos^4 x=\frac{3+4\cos 2x+\cos 4x}{8} $$

Therefore,

$$ I=32\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{3+4\cos 2x+\cos 4x}{8}\,dx $$

$$ I=4\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} (3+4\cos 2x+\cos 4x)\,dx $$

Now integrate termwise:

$$ \int 3\,dx=3x,\qquad \int 4\cos 2x\,dx=2\sin 2x,\qquad \int \cos 4x\,dx=\frac{\sin 4x}{4} $$

So,

$$ I=4\left[3x+2\sin 2x+\frac{\sin 4x}{4}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} $$

At $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$,

$$ 3x=\frac{3\pi}{4},\qquad \sin 2x=\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=1,\qquad \sin 4x=\sin \pi=0 $$

At $x=0$,

$$ 3x=0,\qquad \sin 0=0 $$

Hence,

$$ I=4\left(\frac{3\pi}{4}+2\right) $$

$$ I=3\pi+8 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{3\pi+8} $$

Hence, Option B is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question: The value of the integral $\int\limits_0^2 \frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1})\left(\sqrt{x^4+x^2+1}\right)} \mathrm{d} x$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
$I=\int\limits_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^4+x^2+1}\right)}\right] d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^4+2 x^2+1-x^2}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{\left(x^2+1\right)^2-x^2}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{\left.(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left\{\sqrt{\left(x^2+1+x\right)\left(x^2+1-x\right)}\right\}\right]}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{(\sqrt{x}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2+x+1}\right)}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2+1+x}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2+1-x}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2-x+1}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{(x+1)\left(x^2-x+1\right)}}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x^3+1}}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

Let $x=t^2 \Rightarrow d x=2 t d t$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\int_0^{\sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{2 t^2}{\sqrt{t^6+1}}\right] d t=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \int_0^{\sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{3 t^2}{\sqrt{t^6+1}}\right] d t$

Let $t^3=u \Rightarrow 3 t^2 d t=d u$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 \cdot \int_0^{2 \sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{\boldsymbol{d u}}{\sqrt{u^2+1}}\right]$

Using standard integration formulaes :

$\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\right) \boldsymbol{d x}=\ln \left|\boldsymbol{x}+\sqrt{\boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{a}^2}\right|+C$

= $\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left|\boldsymbol{u}+\sqrt{\boldsymbol{u}^2+1}\right|\right]_0^{2 \sqrt{2}}$

$=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \cdot[\ln (2 \sqrt{2}+3)-\ln 1]=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \cdot[\ln (3+2 \sqrt{2})]$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question: Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{1}{3}, & x \leq \pi / 2 \\ \frac{\mathrm{~b}(1-\sin x)}{(\pi-2 x)^2}, & x>\pi / 2\end{array}\right.$. If $f$ is continuous at $x=\pi / 2$, then the value of $\int\limits_0^{3 \mathrm{~b}-6}\left|x^2+2 x-3\right| \mathrm{d} x$ is :
questions.solution:
The function $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ is continuous at $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}$ where

$\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{1}{3} & ; x \leqslant \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{b(1-\sin x)}{(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 x)^2} & ; x>\frac{\pi}{2}\end{array} \cdots(i)\right.$.

We observe the LHL from equation $(i)$ :

$$ \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{-}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{3} \ldots \ldots(i i) $$

We must have the RHL from equation (i) to be $\frac{1}{3}$ for the function $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ to be continuous at $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}$ :

$$ \lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{x} \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \boldsymbol{f ( x )}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \lim\limits _{\boldsymbol{x} \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{\boldsymbol{b}(1-\sin \boldsymbol{x})}{(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 x)^2}=\frac{1}{3} \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i i i}) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{\boldsymbol{b}(1-\sin \boldsymbol{x})}{(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 \boldsymbol{x})^2}=\frac{0}{0}$ format $\leftarrow$ Apply L'hôpital's rule

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{b \cos \boldsymbol{x}}{4(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 \boldsymbol{x})}=\frac{0}{0}$ format ค Apply L'hôpital's rule once more

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{b \sin x}{8}=\frac{b}{8} \ldots \ldots(i v)$

We can mathematically demonstrate conditional continuity of $f(x)$ at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$ from equation (iii) and (iv) :

$$ \frac{b}{8}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow b=\frac{8}{3} \ldots \ldots(v) $$

Now, I = $\int\limits_0^{3 \mathrm{~b}-6}\left|x^2+2 x-3\right| \mathrm{d} x$

$=\int\limits_0^{(3)\left(\frac{8}{3}\right)-6}\left|\boldsymbol{x}^2+2 \boldsymbol{x}-3\right| \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$=\int\limits_0^2\left|x^2+2 x-3\right| d x$

$=\int\limits_0^2|(x+3)(x-1)| d x$

$\left\{\begin{array}{l}(x+3)(x-1) \geqslant 0 ; x \in(-\infty,-3] \\ (x+3)(x-1) \leqslant 0 ; x \in[-3,1] \\ (x+3)(x-1) \geqslant 0 ; x \in[1, \infty)\end{array}\right.$

$=\int\limits_0^1(x+3)(1-x) d x$ $+\int\limits_1^2(x+3)(x-1) d x$

$=\int\limits_0^1\left(3-2 x-x^2\right) d x$ $+\int\limits_1^2\left(x^2+2 x-3\right) d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =\left[3 x-x^2-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 +\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+x^2-3 x\right]_1^2\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\left(3-1-\frac{1}{3}\right) +\left(\frac{8}{3}+4-6\right)-\left(\frac{1}{3}+1-3\right)\end{aligned}$

= 4

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question: If $\int\limits_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 4}\left(\cot \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cot \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+1\right) d x=\alpha \log _{\mathrm{e}}(\sqrt{3}-1)$, then $9 \alpha^2$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
$\boldsymbol{I}=\int\limits_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\cot \left(\boldsymbol{x}-\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\boldsymbol{x}+\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right)+1\right] \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left|\frac{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right| d x \\ & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left|\frac{\cos \left\{\left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right\}}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right| d x \\ & =\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{2 \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right] d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\left\{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right\}-\left\{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right]} d x\right. \\ & =2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)-\cos (2 x)}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=-\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{-\frac{1}{2}-\cos (2 x)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{1+2 \cos (2 x)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{1+2\left(1-2 \sin ^2 x\right)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{\left.3-4 \sin ^2 x\right)}\right] d x$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3 \sec ^2 x-4 \tan ^2 x}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right)-4 \tan ^2 x}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3-\tan ^2 x}\right] d x$

Substitute $\boldsymbol{t}=\tan \boldsymbol{x} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{t}=\left(\sec ^2 \boldsymbol{x}\right) \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=\int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{2}{3-t^2}\right] d t=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{(\sqrt{3}+t)(\sqrt{3}-t)}\right] d t$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{(\sqrt{3}+t)+(\sqrt{3}-t)}{(\sqrt{3}+t)(\sqrt{3}-t)}\right] d t=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}-t}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}+t}\right] d t$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot[\ln (\sqrt{3}+t)-\ln (\sqrt{3}-t)]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+t}{\sqrt{3}-t}\right)\right]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right)-\ln 2\right]=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left\{\frac{2}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}-\ln 2\right]$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{\frac{2}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{\frac{1}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{(\sqrt{3}-1)^{-2}\right\}=\left(-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln (\sqrt{3}-1) \ldots \ldots(i)$

$$\therefore $$ $ \alpha=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow 9 \alpha^2=12$

questions.question:
Let $y = y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\sec x \dfrac{dy}{dx} - 2y = 2 + 3 \sin x$, $x \in \left(-\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$,

$y(0) = -\dfrac{7}{4}$. Then $y\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}-2 \cos x \cdot y=\cos x(2+3 \sin x) \\ & I \cdot F=e^{-2 \sin x} \\ & G \cdot S y \cdot e^{-2 \sin x}=\int e^{-2 \sin x} \cos x(2+3 \sin x) d x \\ & \text { Put } \begin{array}{l} \sin x=t \\ \cos x \cdot d x=d t \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ y=e^{-2 \sin x}\left(\frac{2+3 \sin x}{-2}-\frac{3}{4}\right)+c $$

When $x=0, c=0$

When $x=\frac{\pi}{6}, y=-\frac{5}{2}$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift
questions.question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution curve of the differential equation $\left(1+x^2\right) \mathrm{d} y+\left(y-\tan ^{-1} x\right) d x=0, y(0)=1$. Then the value of $y(1)$ is :
questions.solution:
Given

$ (1+x^2)\,dy+(y-\tan^{-1}x)\,dx=0,\qquad y(0)=1, $

divide by $dx$:

$ (1+x^2)\frac{dy}{dx}+y-\tan^{-1}x=0 $

$ \frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{1}{1+x^2}y=\frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2}. $

This is linear. Integrating factor:

$ \mu(x)=\exp\!\left(\int \frac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx\right)=e^{\tan^{-1}x}. $

So

$ \frac{d}{dx}\left( y e^{\tan^{-1}x}\right)=e^{\tan^{-1}x}\cdot \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2}. $

Let $t=\tan^{-1}x$, then $dt=\frac{dx}{1+x^2}$. Hence

$ y e^t=\int t e^t\,dt + C = e^t(t-1)+C. $

Therefore

$ y=t-1+Ce^{-t}=\tan^{-1}x-1+Ce^{-\tan^{-1}x}. $

Use $y(0)=1$ and $\tan^{-1}0=0$:

$ 1=0-1+C \implies C=2. $

So

$ y(1)=\frac{\pi}{4}-1+2e^{-\pi/4}=\frac{2}{e^{\pi/4}}+\frac{\pi}{4}-1. $

Correct option: B.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Evening Shift
questions.question: If $y=y(x)$ satisfies the differential equation $16(\sqrt{x+9 \sqrt{x}})(4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}}) \cos y \mathrm{~d} y=(1+2 \sin y) \mathrm{d} x, x>0$ and $y(256)=\frac{\pi}{2}, y(49)=\alpha$, then $2 \sin \alpha$ is equal to :
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\cos y}{1+2 \sin y} d y=\int \frac{1}{16(\sqrt{x+9 \sqrt{x}})(4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}})} d x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 \cos y}{1+2 \sin y} d y=\int \frac{1}{4 t} d t \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Put } 4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}}=t\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}}} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x=d t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \sqrt{x+9 \sqrt{x}}} d x=d t \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log |1+2 \sin y|=\frac{1}{4} \log |t|+\log c \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+2 \sin y}=c t^{1 / 4} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \log |t|+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+2 \sin y}=c t^{1 / 4} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+2 \sin y}=c(4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}})^{1 / 4} \\ & \left(256, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=c(\sqrt{3}) \Rightarrow c=1 \\ & (49, \alpha) \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+2 \sin \alpha}=(1) 8^{1 / 4} \\ & \Rightarrow 1+2 \sin \alpha=2 \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow 2 \sin \alpha=2 \sqrt{2}-1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Morning Shift
questions.question: Let the solution curve of the differential equation $x d y-y d x=\sqrt{x^2+y^2} d x, x>0$, $y(1)=0$, be $y=y(x)$. Then $y(3)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x d y-y d x=\sqrt{x^2+y^2} d x \\ & \frac{x d y-y d x}{x^2}=\frac{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}{x^2} d x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{d(y / x)}{\sqrt{1+(y / x)^2}}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x \\ & \log \left(\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2}\right)=\log x+\log C \\ & y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=x^2 \\ & y-\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=-1 \\ & 2 y=x^2-1 \\ & y(3)=4 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Evening Shift
questions.question:
If the solution curve $y=f(x)$ of the differential equation

$$ \left(x^2-4\right) y^{\prime}-2 x y+2 x\left(4-x^2\right)^2=0, x>2, $$

passes through the point $(3,15)$, then the local maximum value of $f$ is $\_\_\_\_$
questions.solution:
$$ \left(x^2-4\right) y^{\prime}-2 x y+2 x\left(4-x^2\right)^2=0, x>2 $$

rearranging terms.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(x^2-4\right) \frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y=-2 x\left(x^2-4\right)^2 \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{2 x}{x^2-4} y=-2 x\left(x^2-4\right) \end{aligned} $$

this is a linear differential equation of the form $\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q$

$$ P=-\frac{2 x}{x^2-4}, Q=-2 x\left(x^2-4\right) $$

finding integrating factor (I.F.).

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { I.F. } & =e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int-\frac{2 x}{x^2-4} d x} \\ \text { I.F. } & =e^{-\ln \left(x^2-4\right)}=\frac{1}{x^2-4} \end{aligned} $$

the solution is $y \cdot(I . F)=.\int Q \cdot(I . F) d x+$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & y \cdot \frac{1}{x^2-4}=\int-2 x\left(x^2-4\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x^2-4} d x+C \\ & \frac{y}{x^2-4}=\int-2 x d x+C \\ & \frac{y}{x^2-4}=-x^2+C \Rightarrow y=\left(x^2-4\right)\left(C-x^2\right) \end{aligned} $$

substitute point $(3,15)$

$$ 15=\left(3^2-4\right)\left(C-3^2\right) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 15=5(C-9) \Rightarrow 3=C-9 \Rightarrow C=12 \\ & f(x)=\left(x^2-4\right)\left(12-x^2\right) \\ & f(x)=-x^4+16 x^2-48 \end{aligned} $$

finding critical points for local maximum.

$$ \begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=-4 x^3+32 x \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow-4 x\left(x^2-8\right)=0 \\ & \text { since } x>2, x^2=8 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{8}=2 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} $$

$f^{\prime}\left((2 \sqrt{2})^{-}\right)=$positive, $f^{\prime}\left((2 \sqrt{2})^{+}\right)=$negative

so sign of $f^{\prime}(x)$ changing positive to negative about $x=2 \sqrt{2}$ so $x=2 \sqrt{2}$ is point of local maxima

calculating local maximum value.

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(2 \sqrt{2})=\left((2 \sqrt{2})^2-4\right)\left(12-(2 \sqrt{2})^2\right) \\ & =(8-4)(12-8) \\ & =4 \times 4=16 \end{aligned} $$

the local maximum value is 16 .

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Morning Shift
questions.question:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x^4 \mathrm{~d} y+\left(4 x^3 y+2 \sin x\right) \mathrm{d} x=0, x>0, y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0$.

Then $\pi^4 y\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x^4 d y+\left(4 x^3 y+2 \sin x\right) d x=0 \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{4 x^3}{x^4} y=\frac{-2 \sin x}{x^4} \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{4}{x} y=\frac{-2 \sin x}{x^4} \\ & \text { IF }=e^{\int \frac{4}{x} d x=e^{\ln x^4}=x^4} \\ & x^4 \frac{d y}{d x}+4 x^3 y=-2 \sin x \\ & \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^4 y\right)=-2 \sin x \\ & \Rightarrow x^4 y=2 \cos x+c \\ & y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow c=0 \\ & \therefore x^4 y=\cos x \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{2 \cos x}{x^4} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & y\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}{\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^4}=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^4}=\frac{81}{\pi^4} \\ & \pi^4 y\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=81 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Morning Shift
questions.question: Let $f$ be a twice differentiable non-negative function such that $(f(x))^2=25+\int_0^x\left((f(\mathrm{t}))^2+\left(f^{\prime}(\mathrm{t})\right)^2\right) \mathrm{dt}$. Then the mean of $f\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}}(1)\right), f\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}}(2)\right), \ldots . ., f\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}}(625)\right)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
Let $y=f(x)$

$$ y^2=25+\int_0^x\left(f(t)^2+f^{\prime}(t)^2\right) d t $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=y^2+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^2=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y}=d x \\ & \Rightarrow \ln |y|=x+c \end{aligned} $$

Such that

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(0)^2=25+\int_0^0\left(f^2(t)+f^{\prime}(t)^2\right) d t \\ & \Rightarrow f(0)=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \ln 5=c \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \ln |y|=x+\ln 5 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=5 e^x \\ & f(\ln k)=5 e^{\ln k}=5 k \\ & \text { Mean of }\{5,10, \ldots 625 \times 5\} \\ & =\frac{(5+10+\ldots 5 \times 625)}{625}=\frac{5(625)(626)}{625 \times 2} \\ & =5 \times 313 \\ & =1565 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let $y = y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{dy}{dx} - y = x^2 \cot x$, $x \in (0, \pi)$. If $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{2}$, then

$6y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) - 8y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
The given equation is:

$$ x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^2 \cot x $$

To get it into the standard form $\frac{d y}{d x}+P(x) y=Q(x)$, we divide the entire equation by $x$ :

$$ \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} y=x \cot x $$

Here, $P(x)=-\frac{1}{x}$ and $Q(x)=x \cot x$.

Finding the Integrating Factor (I.F.)

The integrating factor is calculated as:

$$ \text { I.F. }=e^{\int P(x) d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\ln x}=e^{\ln \left(x^{-1}\right)}=\frac{1}{x} $$

The solution to the differential equation is:

$$ \begin{gathered} y \cdot(I . F .)=\int Q(x) \cdot(I . F .) d x \\ \frac{y}{x}=\int(x \cot x) \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x \\ \frac{y}{x}=\int \cot x d x \\ \frac{y}{x}=\ln (\sin x)+C \\ y=x \ln (\sin x)+C x \end{gathered} $$

Applying the Initial Condition

We are given $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}$. Plugging these values in:

$$ \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{2} \ln \left(\sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right)+C\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) $$

So, the particular solution is $y=x \ln (\sin x)+x$.

We need to find the value of $6 y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-8 y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$.

  • Calculate $y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ :

$$ y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6} \ln \left(\sin \frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{6} \ln \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{6}(1-\ln 2) $$

  • Calculate $y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ :

$$ y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4} \ln \left(\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4} \ln \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4} \ln \left(2^{-1 / 2}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \ln 2\right) $$

  • Calculate $y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ :

$$ y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4} \ln \left(\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4} \ln \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4} \ln \left(2^{-1 / 2}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \ln 2\right) $$

Now, substitute these into the expression:

$$ \begin{aligned} 6 y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-8 y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) & =6\left[\frac{\pi}{6}(1-\ln 2)\right]-8\left[\frac{\pi}{4}\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \ln 2\right)\right]=\pi(1-\ln 2)-2 \pi\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \ln 2\right) \\ = & \pi-\pi \ln 2-2 \pi+\pi \ln 2=-\pi \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
questions.question:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation

$$ x \frac{d y}{d x}-\sin 2 y=x^3\left(2-x^3\right) \cos ^2 y, x \neq 0 . $$

If $y(2)=0$, then $\tan (y(1))$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given equation is :

$$ x \frac{d y}{d x}-\sin 2 y=x^3\left(2-x^3\right) \cos ^2 y, \quad x \neq 0 $$

Divide by $x \cos ^2 y$

$$ \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{\sin 2 y}{x \cos ^2 y}=x^2\left(2-x^3\right) $$

$$ \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{2 \tan y}{x}=2 x^2-x^5 $$

Substitute $\tan y=t$ and differentiate w.r.t $x$

$$ \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x} $$

Equation (1) becomes linear differential equation.

$\frac{d t}{d x}-\frac{2}{x} t=2 x^2-x^5$

Integrating factor $I F=e^{\int\left(-\frac{2}{x}\right) d x}=e^{-2 \ln x}=e^{\ln \left(\frac{1}{x^2}\right)}=\frac{1}{x^2}$

Solution of linear differential equation :

$$ t \cdot I F=\int(I F)\left(2 x^2-x^5\right) d x $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $t \cdot \frac{1}{x^2}=\int \frac{1}{x^2}\left(2 x^2-x^5\right) d x$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{t}{x^2}=\int\left(2-x^3\right) d x=2 x-\frac{x^4}{4}+C $$

put value of $t=\tan y$

$$ \frac{\tan y}{x^2}=2 x-\frac{x^4}{4}+C $$

using condition $y(2)=0$ to find $C$.

$$ \frac{\tan (0)}{2^2}=2 \times 2-\frac{2^4}{4}+C \Longrightarrow C=0 $$

So, solution of differential equation is

$$ \tan y=2 x^3-\frac{x^6}{4} $$

to calculate $\tan (y(1))$, put $x=1$ in the solution.

$$ \tan (y(1))=2(1)^3-\frac{(1)^6}{4} $$

$=2-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{7}{4}$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let $x = x(y)$ be the solution of the differential equation $2y^2 \frac{dx}{dy} - 2xy + x^2 = 0$, $y > 1$, $x(e) = e$.

Then $x(e^2)$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
$$2y(ydx-xdy)+x^2dy=0$$

$$\Rightarrow -2yx^2\,d\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x^2dy=0$$

$$\Rightarrow -2y\,d\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+dy=0$$

$$\Rightarrow -2d\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\frac{1}{y}dy=0$$

$$\Rightarrow -\frac{2y}{x}+\log_e y=C$$

Given $x(e)=e$

$$\Rightarrow C=-1$$

$$\therefore -\frac{2y}{x}+\log_e y=-1$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{2y}{x}-\log_e y=1$$

Put $y=e^2$, we get

$$\Rightarrow \frac{2e^2}{x}-2=1 \rightarrow \frac{2e^2}{x}=3 \rightarrow x=\frac{2e^2}{3}$$

$$\Rightarrow x(e^2)=\frac{2e^2}{3}$$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
questions.question: If the curve $y = f(x)$ passes through the point $(1, e)$ and satisfies the differential equation $dy = y(2 + \\log_e x) dx$, $x > 0$, then $f(e)$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = y(2+\ln x)$$

To solve this, first separate the variables by dividing both sides by $y$:

$$\int\limits{} \frac{dy}{y} = \int\limits{} (2+\ln x)\,dx$$

Now integrate both sides. The left side gives $\ln y$. For the right side, integrate term by term:

$$\ln y = 2x + x\ln x - x + C$$

Combine like terms $2x-x=x$:

$$\ln y = x + x\ln x + C$$

Since it passes through $(1,e)$, put $x=1$ and $y=e$ in the equation. Then $\ln y=\ln e=1$ and $\ln 1=0$:

$$1 = 1 + 0 + C \rightarrow C = 0$$

So the required relation becomes:

$$\ln y = x + x\ln x$$

Taking antilog (exponential) on both sides:

$$f(x)=y=e^{x+x\ln x}$$

Now substitute $x=e$. Also, $\ln e=1$:

$$\Rightarrow f(e)=e^{e+e}=e^{2e}$$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
questions.question:
Let $y = y(x)$ be the solution curve of the differential equation

$(1 + \sin x)\dfrac{dy}{dx} + (y + 1)\cos x = 0,\ y(0) = 0.$ If the curve $y = y(x)$ passes through the point $\left( \alpha , \dfrac{-1}{2} \right)$,

then a value of $\alpha$ is:
questions.solution:
$$\int \frac{dy}{y+1}=-\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\,dx$$

On the left side, $\int \frac{dy}{y+1}=\ln (y+1)$.

On the right side, take $1+\sin x$ as substitution: let $u=1+\sin x$, then $du=\cos x\,dx$. So, $$-\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\,dx=-\int \frac{du}{u}=-\ln(1+\sin x).$$

So we get $$\Rightarrow \ln (y+1)=-\ln (1+\sin x)+C$$

Now use the initial condition $y(0)=0$. Put $x=0$ and $y=0$ in the equation:

$$\therefore y(0)=0 \rightarrow \ln(0+1)=-\ln(1+\sin 0)+C$$

That is $$0=-\ln(1)+C \Rightarrow C=0.$$

So $$\Rightarrow \ln (y+1)=-\ln (1+\sin x)$$

Taking antilog on both sides, $$\Rightarrow y+1=\frac{1}{1+\sin x}$$

Given that the curve passes through $$\left(\alpha,-\frac{1}{2}\right)$$, substitute $y=-\frac{1}{2}$ and $x=\alpha$:

$$-\frac{1}{2}+1=\frac{1}{1+\sin \alpha}$$

So $$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{1+\sin \alpha} \Rightarrow 1+\sin \alpha=2 \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=1$$

Now $\sin \alpha=1$ gives $$\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ (as per options)}$$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation :

$$ \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{6 x^2+\left(3 x^2+2 x^3+4\right) e^{-2 x}}{\left(x^3+2\right)\left(2+e^{-2 x}\right)}\right) y=2+e^{-2 x} $$

$x \in(-1,2)$, satisfying $y(0)=\frac{3}{2}$. If $y(1)=\alpha\left(2+e^{-2}\right)$, then $\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is

$$ \frac{dy}{dx}+\left(\frac{6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x}}{(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})}\right)y=2+e^{-2x} $$

with initial condition

$$ y(0)=\frac{3}{2}. $$

We have to find $y(1)=\alpha(2+e^{-2})$.

Step 1: Compare with linear differential equation

This is of the form

$$ \frac{dy}{dx}+P(x)y=Q(x), $$

where

$$ P(x)=\frac{6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x}}{(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})}, \qquad Q(x)=2+e^{-2x}. $$

To solve, we find the integrating factor:

$$ \text{I.F.}=e^{\int P(x)\,dx}. $$

Step 2: Simplify $P(x)$

Notice that

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(x^3+2)=3x^2, $$

and

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(2+e^{-2x})=-2e^{-2x}. $$

Now consider

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})\right]. $$

Using product rule,

$$ = (3x^2)(2+e^{-2x})+(x^3+2)(-2e^{-2x}) $$

$$ = 6x^2+3x^2e^{-2x}-2x^3e^{-2x}-4e^{-2x}. $$

This does not match directly. So let us try another observation.

Write numerator as

$$ 6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x}. $$

Now check

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})\right] =6x^2+\left(3x^2-2x^3-4\right)e^{-2x}. $$

Hence if we take

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})^{-1}\right] $$

it becomes complicated. So instead, let us look for a useful factorization.

Observe that

$$ 6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x} =3x^2(2+e^{-2x})+(x^3+2)\cdot 2e^{-2x}. $$

Therefore

$$ P(x)=\frac{3x^2(2+e^{-2x})+(x^3+2)\cdot 2e^{-2x}}{(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})} $$

$$ =\frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}+\frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}. $$

So,

$$ P(x)=\frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}+\frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}. $$

Step 3: Find integrating factor

Now

$$ \int P(x)\,dx=\int \frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}\,dx+\int \frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}\,dx. $$

For the first integral:

Let $u=x^3+2$, then $du=3x^2dx$.

So,

$$ \int \frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}\,dx=\ln(x^3+2). $$

For the second integral, note that

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(2+e^{-2x})=-2e^{-2x}. $$

Hence

$$ \int \frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}\,dx =-\ln(2+e^{-2x}). $$

Therefore,

$$ \int P(x)\,dx=\ln(x^3+2)-\ln(2+e^{-2x}) =\ln\left(\frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\right). $$

So the integrating factor is

$$ \text{I.F.}=e^{\int P(x)\,dx} =\frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}. $$

Step 4: Multiply the equation by the integrating factor

Multiplying throughout by

$$ \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}, $$

we get

$$ \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\frac{dy}{dx} +\frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}P(x)y =(x^3+2). $$

So the left side becomes

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[y\cdot \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\right]. $$

Hence

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[y\cdot \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\right]=x^3+2. $$

Integrating both sides,

$$ y\cdot \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}} =\int (x^3+2)\,dx =\frac{x^4}{4}+2x+C. $$

Thus,

$$ y=\frac{2+e^{-2x}}{x^3+2}\left(\frac{x^4}{4}+2x+C\right). $$

Step 5: Use initial condition

Given

$$ y(0)=\frac{3}{2}. $$

Substitute $x=0$:

$$ \frac{3}{2} =\frac{2+e^{0}}{0^3+2}\left(0+0+C\right) =\frac{3}{2}C. $$

So,

$$ C=1. $$

Hence solution is

$$ y=\frac{2+e^{-2x}}{x^3+2}\left(\frac{x^4}{4}+2x+1\right). $$

Step 6: Find $y(1)$

Substitute $x=1$:

$$ y(1)=\frac{2+e^{-2}}{1^3+2}\left(\frac{1}{4}+2+1\right). $$

$$ y(1)=\frac{2+e^{-2}}{3}\left(\frac{1}{4}+3\right) =\frac{2+e^{-2}}{3}\cdot \frac{13}{4} $$

$$ y(1)=\frac{13}{12}(2+e^{-2}). $$

Given that

$$ y(1)=\alpha(2+e^{-2}), $$

so

$$ \alpha=\frac{13}{12}. $$

Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\frac{13}{12}} $$

So, the correct option is D.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let $f$ be a twice differentiable function such that

$$ f(x)=\int_0^x \tan (t-x) d t-\int_0^x f(t) \tan t d t, x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) $$

Then $f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+12 f^{\prime}\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is equal to _______.
questions.solution:
Let

$$ f(x)=\int_0^x \tan (t-x)\,dt-\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt, \qquad x\in \left(-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}\right). $$

We have to find

$$ f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+12f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right). $$

Step 1: Simplify the first integral

Consider

$$ \int_0^x \tan(t-x)\,dt. $$

Put

$$ u=t-x \implies du=dt. $$

When $t=0$, $u=-x$, and when $t=x$, $u=0$.

So,

$$ \int_0^x \tan(t-x)\,dt=\int_{-x}^0 \tan u\,du. $$

Now,

$$ \int \tan u\,du = -\ln(\cos u). $$

Hence,

$$ \int_{-x}^0 \tan u\,du = \left[-\ln(\cos u)\right]_{-x}^0 = -\ln(\cos 0)+\ln(\cos(-x)). $$

Since $\cos 0=1$ and $\cos(-x)=\cos x$,

$$ \int_0^x \tan(t-x)\,dt=\ln(\cos x). $$

Therefore the given equation becomes

$$ f(x)=\ln(\cos x)-\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt. $$

So,

$$ f(x)+\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt=\ln(\cos x). $$

Step 2: Differentiate the equation

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$.

Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt\right)=f(x)\tan x. $$

Also,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\big(\ln(\cos x)\big)= -\tan x. $$

Thus,

$$ f'(x)+f(x)\tan x=-\tan x. $$

So,

$$ f'(x)=-(f(x)+1)\tan x. $$

Step 3: Find $f(x)$ explicitly

Now observe that $f(x)=-1$ satisfies the differential equation:

$$ f'(x)=0,\qquad -(f(x)+1)\tan x=0. $$

Also from the original equation, put $x=0$:

$$ f(0)=\int_0^0 \tan(t-0)\,dt-\int_0^0 f(t)\tan t\,dt=0. $$

So,

$$ f(0)=0. $$

Let us solve the differential equation properly.

From

$$ f'(x)+(f(x)+1)\tan x=0, $$

put

$$ y(x)=f(x)+1. $$

Then

$$ y'(x)+y(x)\tan x=0. $$

This gives

$$ \frac{y'}{y}=-\tan x. $$

Integrating,

$$ \ln |y|=\int -\tan x\,dx=\ln(\cos x)+C. $$

Hence,

$$ y=C\cos x. $$

Therefore,

$$ f(x)+1=C\cos x. $$

Using $f(0)=0$,

$$ 0+1=C\cos 0=C. $$

So $C=1$, and hence

$$ f(x)+1=\cos x $$

which gives

$$ f(x)=\cos x-1. $$

Step 4: Find derivatives

Since

$$ f(x)=\cos x-1, $$

we get

$$ f'(x)=-\sin x, $$

and

$$ f''(x)=-\cos x. $$

Now compute required values:

1. $f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$$ f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) =-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}. $$

2. $f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$$ f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\sin\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) =\frac{1}{2}. $$

So,

$$ 12f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=12\cdot \frac{1}{2}=6. $$

3. $f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$$ f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-1 =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-1. $$

Step 5: Add them

$$ f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+12f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) $$

$$ = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+6+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-1\right) $$

$$ = 6-1=5. $$

Hence, the required value is

$$ \boxed{5}. $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(1+x+x^2\right)\left(1-y+y^2\right), y(0)=\frac{1}{2}$. Then $(2 y(1)-1)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given

$$ \frac{dy}{dx}=(1+x+x^2)(1-y+y^2), \qquad y(0)=\frac12 $$

We will solve it by the method of separation of variables.

Since

$$ \frac{dy}{1-y+y^2}=(1+x+x^2)\,dx $$

Now simplify the denominator on the left:

$$ 1-y+y^2=y^2-y+1=\left(y-\frac12\right)^2+\frac34 $$

So,

$$ \int \frac{dy}{\left(y-\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\right)^2} = \int (1+x+x^2)\,dx $$

Using the standard result

$$ \int \frac{du}{u^2+a^2}=\frac1a \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{u}{a}\right) $$

we get

$$ \frac{2}{\sqrt3}\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2y-1}{\sqrt3}\right) = x+\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^3}{3}+C $$

Now use the initial condition $y(0)=\frac12$.

Then

$$ 2y-1=0 $$

so

$$ \frac{2}{\sqrt3}\tan^{-1}(0)=C \Rightarrow C=0 $$

Hence,

$$ \frac{2}{\sqrt3}\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2y-1}{\sqrt3}\right) = x+\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^3}{3} $$

At $x=1$,

$$ x+\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^3}{3} = 1+\frac12+\frac13 = \frac{11}{6} $$

Therefore,

$$ \frac{2}{\sqrt3}\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2y(1)-1}{\sqrt3}\right)=\frac{11}{6} $$

So

$$ \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2y(1)-1}{\sqrt3}\right)=\frac{11\sqrt3}{12} $$

Taking tangent on both sides,

$$ \frac{2y(1)-1}{\sqrt3}=\tan\left(\frac{11\sqrt3}{12}\right) $$

Hence,

$$ 2y(1)-1=\sqrt3 \tan\left(\frac{11\sqrt3}{12}\right) $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\sqrt3 \tan\left(\frac{11\sqrt3}{12}\right)} $$

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Let $f:[1, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a differentiable function defined as $f(x)=\int_1^x f(\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt}+(1-x)\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x-1\right)+\mathrm{e}$.

Then the value of $f(f(1))$ is :
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x)=\int_1^x f(t)\,dt +(1-x)(\ln x-1)+e,\qquad x\in[1,\infty) $$

We have to find $f(f(1))$.

First, put $x=1$ in the given equation:

$$ f(1)=\int_1^1 f(t)\,dt +(1-1)(\ln 1-1)+e $$

Now,

$$ \int_1^1 f(t)\,dt=0,\qquad (1-1)(\ln 1-1)=0 $$

So,

$$ f(1)=e $$

Hence,

$$ f(f(1))=f(e) $$

Now we find $f(x)$.

Differentiate both sides of

$$ f(x)=\int_1^x f(t)\,dt +(1-x)(\ln x-1)+e $$

Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_1^x f(t)\,dt\right)=f(x) $$

So,

$$ f'(x)=f(x)+\frac{d}{dx}\big[(1-x)(\ln x-1)\big] $$

Now differentiate the second term carefully:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\big[(1-x)(\ln x-1)\big] =-(\ln x-1)+(1-x)\cdot \frac{1}{x} $$

Simplifying,

$$ = -\ln x+1+\frac{1-x}{x} $$

$$ = -\ln x+1+\frac{1}{x}-1 $$

$$ = \frac{1}{x}-\ln x $$

Therefore,

$$ f'(x)=f(x)+\frac{1}{x}-\ln x $$

So,

$$ f'(x)-f(x)=\frac{1}{x}-\ln x $$

Now let us check whether

$$ f(x)=\ln x+1 $$

satisfies this equation.

Then

$$ f'(x)=\frac{1}{x} $$

So,

$$ f'(x)-f(x)=\frac{1}{x}-(\ln x+1) $$

This does not match directly. So instead, let us use the original equation to test $f(x)=\ln x+1$.

Right-hand side:

$$ \int_1^x (\ln t+1)\,dt +(1-x)(\ln x-1)+e $$

Now,

$$ \int (\ln t+1)\,dt=t\ln t $$

Therefore,

$$ \int_1^x (\ln t+1)\,dt=x\ln x-1\cdot \ln 1=x\ln x $$

since $\ln 1=0$.

Thus RHS becomes

$$ x\ln x +(1-x)(\ln x-1)+e $$

Expand:

$$ = x\ln x +(1-x)\ln x -(1-x)+e $$

$$ = \ln x -1 +x +e $$

This is not equal to $\ln x+1$, so this trial is not correct.

Now solve the differential equation properly:

$$ f'(x)-f(x)=\frac{1}{x}-\ln x $$

This is a linear differential equation.

Its integrating factor is

$$ I.F.=e^{-x} $$

Multiplying both sides by $e^{-x}$,

$$ e^{-x}f'(x)-e^{-x}f(x)=e^{-x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\ln x\right) $$

So,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(f(x)e^{-x}\right)=e^{-x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\ln x\right) $$

This looks difficult, so let us instead use the original functional equation more intelligently.

Write

$$ g(x)=f(x)-(\ln x+1) $$

Then

$$ f(x)=g(x)+\ln x+1 $$

Substitute into the original equation:

$$ g(x)+\ln x+1=\int_1^x \big(g(t)+\ln t+1\big)\,dt +(1-x)(\ln x-1)+e $$

Now,

$$ \int_1^x (\ln t+1)\,dt=x\ln x $$

Hence,

$$ g(x)+\ln x+1=\int_1^x g(t)\,dt +x\ln x +(1-x)(\ln x-1)+e $$

Simplify the terms:

$$ x\ln x +(1-x)(\ln x-1) = x\ln x +(1-x)\ln x -(1-x) $$

$$ = \ln x -1 +x $$

So,

$$ g(x)+\ln x+1=\int_1^x g(t)\,dt +\ln x -1 +x +e $$

Thus,

$$ g(x)+1=\int_1^x g(t)\,dt -1 +x +e $$

This is not simplifying nicely. Let us now directly guess from options.

Since

$$ f(1)=e $$

we need $f(e)$.

Use the original equation at $x=e$:

$$ f(e)=\int_1^e f(t)\,dt +(1-e)(\ln e-1)+e $$

But

$$ \ln e=1 $$

so

$$ (1-e)(\ln e-1)=0 $$

Hence

$$ f(e)=\int_1^e f(t)\,dt +e $$

Now from the given equation,

$$ f'(x)=f(x)+\frac{1}{x}-\ln x $$

Try

$$ f(x)=\ln x+1+Ce^x $$

Then

$$ f'(x)=\frac{1}{x}+Ce^x $$

So,

$$ f'(x)-f(x)=\frac{1}{x}+Ce^x-(\ln x+1+Ce^x) =\frac{1}{x}-\ln x-1 $$

We are short by $-1$. So modify the trial.

Try

$$ f(x)=\ln x + A + Ce^x $$

Then

$$ f'(x)=\frac{1}{x}+Ce^x $$

Hence

$$ f'(x)-f(x)=\frac{1}{x}+Ce^x-(\ln x + A +Ce^x) =\frac{1}{x}-\ln x-A $$

Comparing with

$$ \frac{1}{x}-\ln x $$

we get

$$ A=0 $$

So,

$$ f(x)=\ln x+Ce^x $$

Now use $f(1)=e$:

$$ f(1)=\ln 1+Ce=e $$

$$ 0+Ce=e $$

So,

$$ C=1 $$

Therefore,

$$ f(x)=\ln x+e^x $$

Now,

$$ f(e)=\ln e+e^e=1+e^e $$

Hence,

$$ f(f(1))=1+e^e $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{1+e^e} $$

Therefore, Option A is correct.
