questions.question:
Consider the following aqueous solutions.

I. 2.2 g Glucose in 125 mL of solution.

II. 1.9 g Calcium chloride in 250 mL of solution.

III. 9.0 g Urea in 500 mL of solution.

IV. 20.5 g Aluminium sulphate in 750 mL of solution.

The correct increasing order of boiling point of these solutions will be :

[Given : Molar mass in g mol−1 : H = 1, C = 12, N = 14, O = 16, Cl = 35.5, Ca = 40, Al = 27 and S = 32]
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{i} \cdot \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{~m}\\ &\text { For dilute solution }(M=m) \end{aligned} $$

$$\text{We know that boiling point elevation depends on } \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}.$$

$$\text{Since } \mathrm{k}_\mathrm{b} \text{ is same for water in all cases, we compare only } \mathrm{i}\times m \text{ (or } \mathrm{i}\times M\text{ for dilute solutions).}$$

$$
\text { Molarity }
$$		 $$
\mathbf{i} \times \mathbf{m}
$$
$$
\text { (I) } \mathrm{M}_{\text {glucose }}=\frac{2.2}{180} \times \frac{1000}{125}=0.098
$$		 $$
0.098 \times 1
$$
$$
\text { (II) } \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{CaCl}_2}=\frac{1.9}{111} \times \frac{1000}{250}=0.068
$$		 $$
0.068 \times 3
$$
$$
\text { (III) } \mathrm{M}_{\text {urea }}=\frac{9}{60} \times \frac{1000}{500}=0.3
$$		 $$
0.3 \times 1
$$
$$
\text { (IV) } \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_2}=\frac{20.5}{342} \times \frac{1000}{750} \simeq 0.08
$$		 $$
0.08 \times 5
$$

$$\text{Now compare the values of } \mathrm{i}\times m:$$

$$\text{(I) Glucose: } 0.098\times 1=0.098$$

$$\text{(II) } \mathrm{CaCl}_2\text{: } 0.068\times 3=0.204$$

$$\text{(III) Urea: } 0.3\times 1=0.3$$

$$\text{(IV) Aluminium sulphate: } 0.08\times 5=0.4$$

Order of $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3>$ Urea $>\mathrm{CaCl}_2>$ Glucose

So order of BP $=\mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3>$ Urea $>\mathrm{CaCl}_2>$ Glucose

So Answer will be I $<$ II $<$ III $<$ IV

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
questions.question: At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}), 2$ moles of liquid A and 3 moles of liquid B are mixed. The vapour pressure of ideal solution formed is 320 mm Hg . At this stage, one mole of A and one mole of B are added to the solution. The vapour pressure is now measured as 328.6 mm Hg . The vapour pressure (in mm Hg ) of A and B are respectively:
questions.solution:
For an ideal solution, by Raoult’s law:

$$P_{\text{total}}=x_A P_A^0 + x_B P_B^0$$

where $P_A^0,\;P_B^0$ are vapour pressures of pure liquids A and B.

Step 1: First mixture (2 mol A + 3 mol B)

Total moles $=2+3=5$

$$x_A=\frac{2}{5},\quad x_B=\frac{3}{5}$$

Given $P_{\text{total}}=320$ mm Hg:

$$320=\frac{2}{5}P_A^0+\frac{3}{5}P_B^0$$

Multiply by 5:

$$1600=2P_A^0+3P_B^0 \quad ...(1)$$

Step 2: After adding 1 mol A and 1 mol B

New moles: A $=3$, B $=4$, total $=7$

$$x_A=\frac{3}{7},\quad x_B=\frac{4}{7}$$

Given new vapour pressure $=328.6$ mm Hg:

$$328.6=\frac{3}{7}P_A^0+\frac{4}{7}P_B^0$$

Multiply by 7:

$$2300.2=3P_A^0+4P_B^0 \quad ...(2)$$

Step 3: Solve the two equations

From (1): $2P_A^0+3P_B^0=1600$

From (2): $3P_A^0+4P_B^0=2300.2$

Multiply (1) by 3:

$$6P_A^0+9P_B^0=4800$$

Multiply (2) by 2:

$$6P_A^0+8P_B^0=4600.4$$

Subtract:

$$P_B^0=4800-4600.4=199.6 \approx 200$$

Put in (1):

$$2P_A^0+3(199.6)=1600$$

$$2P_A^0+598.8=1600$$

$$2P_A^0=1001.2 \Rightarrow P_A^0=500.6 \approx 500$$

Final Answer

Vapour pressures of A and B are approximately:

$$P_A^0=500\ \text{mm Hg},\quad P_B^0=200\ \text{mm Hg}$$

Correct option: C (500, 200)

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Solution A is prepared by dissolving 1 g of a protein (molar mass = 50,000 g mol-1) in 0.5 L of water at 300 K. Its osmotic pressure is $x$ bar. Solution B is made by dissolving 2 g of the same protein in 1 L of water at 300 K. Osmotic pressure of solution B is $y$ bar. Entire solution of A is mixed with entire solution of B at the same temperature. The osmotic pressure of resultant solution is $z$ bar. $x$, $y$ and $z$ respectively are:
(R = 0.083 L bar mol-1 K-1)
questions.solution:
For osmotic pressure, we use the NCERT relation:

$$ \pi = C R T = \frac{n}{V}RT $$

where

  • $\pi$ = osmotic pressure

  • $n$ = number of moles of solute

  • $V$ = volume of solution

  • $R = 0.083 \, \text{L bar mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$

  • $T = 300 \, \text{K}$

Protein molar mass is:

$$ M = 50000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Now solve each part.

For solution A

Mass of protein $= 1 \, \text{g}$

Moles of protein:

$$ n_A = \frac{1}{50000} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} $$

Volume:

$$ V_A = 0.5 \, \text{L} $$

So concentration:

$$ C_A = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} $$

Hence osmotic pressure:

$$ x = \pi_A = C_A RT $$

$$ x = (4 \times 10^{-5})(0.083)(300) $$

First calculate:

$$ 0.083 \times 300 = 24.9 $$

So,

$$ x = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 24.9 = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

For solution B

Mass of protein $= 2 \, \text{g}$

Moles:

$$ n_B = \frac{2}{50000} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} $$

Volume:

$$ V_B = 1 \, \text{L} $$

Concentration:

$$ C_B = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{1} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} $$

Thus,

$$ y = \pi_B = C_BRT $$

$$ y = (4 \times 10^{-5})(0.083)(300) $$

$$ y = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

After mixing A and B

Total moles of protein:

$$ n_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 10^{-5} + 4 \times 10^{-5} = 6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} $$

Total volume:

$$ V_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5 \, \text{L} $$

Concentration after mixing:

$$ C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} $$

Again,

$$ z = \pi_{\text{mix}} = C_{\text{mix}}RT $$

$$ z = (4 \times 10^{-5})(0.083)(300) = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

So,

$$ x = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar}, \quad y = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar}, \quad z = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
questions.question:
19.5 g of fluoro acetic acid (molar mass = 78 g mol−1) is dissolved in 500 g of water at 298 K. The depression in the freezing point of water was 1∘C. What is $K_a$ of fluoro acetic acid?

(For water, $K_f = 1.86$ K kg mol−1). Assume molarity and molality to have same values.
questions.solution:
Given:

  • Mass of fluoroacetic acid $= 19.5 \, \text{g}$

  • Molar mass $= 78 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$

  • Mass of water $= 500 \, \text{g} = 0.5 \, \text{kg}$

  • Depression in freezing point, $\Delta T_f = 1^\circ \text{C} = 1 \, \text{K}$

  • $K_f = 1.86 \, \text{K kg mol}^{-1}$

We have to find $K_a$ of fluoroacetic acid.

First, calculate the number of moles of acid:

$$ n = \frac{19.5}{78} = 0.25 \, \text{mol} $$

Now, molality of the solution:

$$ m = \frac{0.25}{0.5} = 0.5 \, \text{mol kg}^{-1} $$

For an electrolyte,

$$ \Delta T_f = i K_f m $$

So,

$$ 1 = i \times 1.86 \times 0.5 $$

$$ 1 = i \times 0.93 $$

$$ i = \frac{1}{0.93} \approx 1.075 $$

Fluoroacetic acid is a weak monoprotic acid:

$$ \text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^- $$

If degree of dissociation is $\alpha$, then for this type of acid:

$$ i = 1 + \alpha $$

Therefore,

$$ \alpha = i - 1 = 1.075 - 1 = 0.075 $$

Now use Ostwald’s dilution law:

$$ K_a = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha} $$

Given in the question, assume molarity and molality are same, so

$$ C = 0.5 $$

Thus,

$$ K_a = \frac{0.5 \times (0.075)^2}{1-0.075} $$

$$ = \frac{0.5 \times 0.005625}{0.925} $$

$$ = \frac{0.0028125}{0.925} \approx 3.04 \times 10^{-3} $$

So,

$$ K_a \approx 3 \times 10^{-3} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{3 \times 10^{-3}} $$

Option D.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
questions.question: At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 0.1 \mathrm{M}, 1 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ aqueous solution and $0.1 \mathrm{M}, 1 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{FeCl}_3$ aqueous solution are placed in a container separated by a semi permeable membrane AB . Assume complete dissociation of both the solutes. Which of the following statement is correct?
options: Blue color is formed on both sides.
Ionic solutes in aqueous solution can pass through semi-permeable membrane.
To cause the reverse flow of solvent during osmosis, external pressure (any value) should be applied to side ' $x$ '.
questions.solution:
(A) A semi-permeable membrane (SPM) allows only solvent molecules (water) to pass through it. It does not allow ions to pass.

So, the solute ions from the two sides cannot come together. Therefore, no blue colour (due to mixing of ions) will appear on either side.

(B) Because ions cannot cross the SPM, only solvent molecules can move from one side to the other depending on osmotic pressure.

(C) A hypotonic solution is the one which has lower osmotic pressure (lower “effective” concentration of solute particles).

We use the NCERT relation for osmotic pressure:

As osmotic pressure $(\pi)=i C R T$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6^{-4} \quad(\because i=5) \\ & \mathrm{FeCl}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{+3}+\mathrm{3Cl^-} \quad(\because i=4) \\ & \text { for side } x: \pi=5 \mathrm{CRT}=0.5 \mathrm{RT} \quad(\because c=0.1 \mathrm{~m}) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, y: \pi=0.4 R T \quad(\because c=0.1 \mathrm{~m}) \end{aligned} $$

effective concentration of $x=\dot{i} \times C=0.5 \mathrm{M}$

$$ y=i \times C=0.4 \mathrm{~m} $$

Since, effective concentration of $y$ is less than $x$. Hence, $y$ is hypotonic.

(D) In reverse osmosis, pressure is applied on the side having higher osmotic pressure (here, side $x$). But this applied external pressure must be greater than the osmotic pressure difference between the two sides.

i.e. P applied > $\left(\pi_x-\pi_y\right)$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
A non-volatile, non-electrolyte solid solute when dissolved in 40 g of a solvent, the vapour pressure of the solvent decreased from 760 mm Hg to 750 mm Hg . If the same solution boils at 320 K , then the number of moles of the solvent present in the solution is $\_\_\_\_$ . (Nearest integer)

[Given: boiling point of the pure solvent $=319.5 \mathrm{~K}$,

$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of the solvent $=0.3 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ]
questions.solution:
For a non-volatile, non-electrolyte solute, first use relative lowering of vapour pressure:

$$ \frac{p^0-p}{p^0}=x_2 $$

where $x_2$ is the mole fraction of solute.

Given:

$$ p^0=760\ \text{mm Hg}, \quad p=750\ \text{mm Hg} $$

So,

$$ x_2=\frac{760-750}{760}=\frac{10}{760}=\frac{1}{76} $$

Now,

$$ x_2=\frac{n_2}{n_1+n_2} $$

where $n_1$ = moles of solvent and $n_2$ = moles of solute.

Hence,

$$ \frac{n_2}{n_1+n_2}=\frac{1}{76} $$

This gives

$$ 76n_2=n_1+n_2 $$

$$ n_1=75n_2 $$

Now use elevation in boiling point:

$$ \Delta T_b = K_b m $$

Given:

$$ \Delta T_b=320-319.5=0.5\ \text{K} $$

and

$$ K_b=0.3\ \text{K kg mol}^{-1} $$

So molality,

$$ m=\frac{\Delta T_b}{K_b}=\frac{0.5}{0.3}=\frac{5}{3} $$

But

$$ m=\frac{n_2}{\text{mass of solvent in kg}} $$

Mass of solvent $=40\text{ g}=0.040\text{ kg}$

Thus,

$$ \frac{n_2}{0.040}=\frac{5}{3} $$

$$ n_2=\frac{5}{3}\times 0.040=0.0667\ \text{mol} $$

Now,

$$ n_1=75n_2=75\times 0.0667=5.00\ \text{mol} $$

Therefore, the number of moles of solvent in the solution is

$$ \boxed{5} $$

Nearest integer: $\boxed{5}$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
20 g hemoglobin in a 1 L aqueous solution $(\mathrm{A})$ at 300 K is separated from pure water by semi permeable membrane. At equilibrium the height of solution in a tube dipped in a solution (A) is found to be 80.0 mm higher than the tube dipped in water.

The molar mass of hemoglobin is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. (Nearest integer)

(Given : $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}, \mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{kPa} \mathrm{dm} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, density of solution $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ )
questions.solution:
For a solution separated from pure water by a semipermeable membrane, at equilibrium the osmotic pressure is balanced by the hydrostatic pressure.

So,

$$ \pi = h \rho g $$

Given:

  • $h = 80.0 \, \text{mm} = 0.080 \, \text{m}$

  • $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg m}^{-3}$

  • $g = 10 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$

Therefore,

$$ \pi = 0.080 \times 1000 \times 10 $$

$$ \pi = 800 \, \text{Pa} = 0.8 \, \text{kPa} $$

Now, using the osmotic pressure formula:

$$ \pi = CRT $$

where $C = \dfrac{n}{V}$

So,

$$ \pi = \frac{w}{MV}RT $$

Here,

  • $w = 20 \, \text{g}$

  • $M =$ molar mass

  • $V = 1 \, \text{L} = 1 \, \text{dm}^3$

  • $R = 8.3 \, \text{kPa dm}^3 \text{K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$

  • $T = 300 \, \text{K}$

  • $\pi = 0.8 \, \text{kPa}$

Substituting,

$$ 0.8 = \frac{20}{M \times 1}\times 8.3 \times 300 $$

$$ 0.8 = \frac{20 \times 8.3 \times 300}{M} $$

$$ M = \frac{20 \times 8.3 \times 300}{0.8} $$

$$ M = 62250 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Convert into $\text{kg mol}^{-1}$:

$$ M = 62.25 \, \text{kg mol}^{-1} $$

Nearest integer:

$$ \boxed{62 \, \text{kg mol}^{-1}} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : $\quad \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ molecules move from the chamber 1 to chamber 2 .

Statement II : The osmotic pressure of a solution prepared by dissolving 50 mg of potassium sulphate (molar mass $=174 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) in 2 L of water (at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) is 0.0107 bar. (Given: $\mathrm{R}=0.083 \mathrm{dm}^3$ bar $\mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and assume complete dissociation of electrolyte)

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

Both Statement I and Statement II are false

Statement I is true but Statement II is false

Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.solution:
In chamber (I), the concentration of solute is calculated as:

$$C_1 = \frac{18/180}{0.1} = 1\, \text{M}$$

In chamber (II), the concentration is given by:

$$C_2 = \frac{30/180}{250/1000} = \frac{2}{3}\, \text{M}$$

Since osmotic pressure depends on concentration and $\pi_1 > \pi_2$, water will move from the side having lower osmotic pressure (chamber II) to the side having higher osmotic pressure (chamber I).

Osmotic pressure is calculated using the formula:

$$\pi = iCRT$$

For potassium sulfate $(\mathrm{K_2SO_4})$, the van’t Hoff factor is $i = 3$.

So,

$$\pi = 3 \times \left(\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}/174}{2}\right) \times 0.083 \times 300$$

On solving,

$$\pi = 0.0107\, \text{bar}$$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
questions.question: When 0.25 moles of a non-volatile, non-ionizable solute was dissolved in 1 mole of a solvent the vapor pressure of solution was $x \%$ of vapor pressure of pure solvent. What is $x \%$ ?
questions.solution:
Let the mole fraction of the solvent be $ x_1 $ and that of the solute be $ x_2 $.

Given:

Moles of solute $ n_2 = 0.25 $

Moles of solvent $ n_1 = 1 $

$ x_1 = \frac{n_1}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.25} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8 $

$ x_2 = 1 - x_1 = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 $

For a non-volatile solute, the vapor pressure of the solution is given by:

$ P_{\text{solution}} = x_1 P^0 $

where $ P^0 $ is the vapor pressure of the pure solvent.

$ \frac{P_{\text{solution}}}{P^0} \times 100 = x_1 \times 100 = 0.8 \times 100 = 80\% $

Final Answer:

$ x = 80\% $

Option D: 80%

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Given below are two statements :

Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{Cl}$ are $12,1,16$ and $35.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, respectively

Statement I : In $30 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ solution of methanol in $\mathrm{CCl}_4($ at T K$)$, the mole fraction of $\mathrm{CCl}_4$ is equal to 0.33 .

Statement II : Mixture of methanol and $\mathrm{CCl}_4$ shows positive deviation from Raoult's law. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

Both Statement I and Statement II are false

Statement I is true but Statement II is false

Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.solution:
• Molar masses:

  • C = 12 g mol⁻¹

  • H = 1 g mol⁻¹

  • O = 16 g mol⁻¹

  • Cl = 35.5 g mol⁻¹

Formula masses:

  • For methanol, CH₃OH:

    $ M = 12 + (3 \times 1) + 16 + 1 = 32\ \text{g mol}^{-1} $

  • For carbon tetrachloride, CCl₄:

    $ M = 12 + (4 \times 35.5) = 12 + 142 = 154\ \text{g mol}^{-1} $

This means in 100 g of solution:

  • Mass of methanol = 30 g

  • Mass of CCl₄ = 70 g

$ n_{\text{methanol}} = \frac{30}{32} = 0.9375\ \text{mol} $

$ n_{\text{CCl}_4} = \frac{70}{154} = 0.4545\ \text{mol} $

Total moles = $ 0.9375 + 0.4545 = 1.392 $

So,

$ x_{\text{methanol}} = \frac{0.9375}{1.392} = 0.674 $

$ x_{\text{CCl}_4} = \frac{0.4545}{1.392} = 0.326 \approx 0.33 $

Hence, Statement I is correct.

  • Methanol is polar and forms hydrogen bonds.

  • Carbon tetrachloride is non-polar.

  • When they are mixed, the H-bonding between methanol molecules weakens (since CCl₄ cannot form hydrogen bonds with methanol).

    → The intermolecular attraction between unlike molecules decreases.

As a result, the escaping tendency of molecules increases and vapour pressure increases, leading to positive deviation from Raoult’s law.

Hence, Statement II is also correct.

Final Answer

Both Statement I and Statement II are true.

$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Which of the following statements are not correct?

A. For water, magnitude of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ is more than the magnitude of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$.

B. The elevation in boiling point of water when a non-volatile solute is added to it is larger in magnitude than its depression in freezing point.

C. Osmotic pressure measurement is preferred over any other colligative property to determine molar mass of proteins and polymers.

$$ \text { D. The dimerised form of benzoic acid in benzene is } $$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
A and B only

A and D only

A, B and D only

A, C and D only
questions.solution:
(A) $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for water $=0.512 \frac{{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Kg}}{\mathrm{mol}}$

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \text { for water }=1.86 \frac{{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Kg}}{\mathrm{~mol}} $$

So, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}<\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water

(B) Since, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}<\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ so $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}<\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}$ [same equimolal solution]

(C) Osmotic pressure is used to determine molar mass of protiens \& polymers

(D) dimerised form of benzoic acid in benzene

Option (A), (B) & (D) are incorrect

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Evening Shift
questions.question:
By usual analysis, 1.00 g of compound (X) gave 1.79 g of magnesium pyrophosphate. The percentage of phosphorus in compound (X) is : (nearest integer)

(Given, molar mass in g mol−1: O = 16, Mg = 24, P = 31)
questions.solution:
The chemical formula for magnesium pyrophosphate is $Mg_2P_2O_7$.

Using the given molar masses (Mg = 24, P = 31, O = 16):

$$ \text{Molar mass of } Mg_2P_2O_7 = (2 \times 24) + (2 \times 31) + (7 \times 16) $$

$$ \text{Molar mass} = 48 + 62 + 112 = 222 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

One mole of $Mg_2P_2O_7$ contains 2 moles of phosphorus atoms.

$$ \text{Mass of P} = 2 \times 31 = 62 \text{ g} $$

This means that 222 g of $Mg_2P_2O_7$ contains 62 g of phosphorus.

The mass of $Mg_2P_2O_7$ obtained in the experiment is 1.79 g.

Applying the unitary method:

$$ \text{Mass of P in 1.79 g of } Mg_2P_2O_7 = \frac{62}{222} \times 1.79 \text{ g} $$

$$ \text{Mass of P} = \frac{110.98}{222} \approx 0.4999 \text{ g} $$

The percentage of an element in a given compound is calculated as:

$$ \% \text{ of P} = \frac{\text{Mass of P}}{\text{Total mass of compound (X)}} \times 100 $$

$$ \% \text{ of P} = \frac{0.4999 \text{ g}}{1.00 \text{ g}} \times 100 = 49.99\% $$

Rounding off to the nearest integer, we get 50%.

Option D is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Evening Shift
questions.question: Aqueous HCl reacts with MnO2(s) to form MnCl2(aq), Cl2(g), and H2O(l). What is the weight (in g) of Cl2 liberated when 8.7 g of MnO2(s) is reacted with excess aqueous HCl solution? (Given Molar mass in g mol−1 Mn = 55, Cl = 35.5, O = 16, H = 1)
questions.solution:
Balance the reaction first (NCERT method):

$$\text{MnO}_2(s) + 4\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MnCl}_2(aq) + \text{Cl}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

Step 1: Calculate moles of $\text{MnO}_2$

Molar mass of $\text{MnO}_2$:

$$M(\text{MnO}_2)=55 + 2(16)=55+32=87\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

Moles of $\text{MnO}_2$ in $8.7\ \text{g}$:

$$n(\text{MnO}_2)=\frac{8.7}{87}=0.1\ \text{mol}$$

Step 2: Use mole ratio to find moles of $\text{Cl}_2$

From the balanced equation:

$$1\ \text{mol MnO}_2 \rightarrow 1\ \text{mol Cl}_2$$

So,

$$n(\text{Cl}_2)=0.1\ \text{mol}$$

Step 3: Convert moles of $\text{Cl}_2$ to mass

Molar mass of $\text{Cl}_2$:

$$M(\text{Cl}_2)=2\times 35.5=71\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

Mass of $\text{Cl}_2$:

$$m(\text{Cl}_2)=n\times M = 0.1 \times 71 = 7.1\ \text{g}$$

Answer: $7.1\ \text{g}$ (Option C)

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift
questions.question:
14.0 g of calcium metal is allowed to react with excess HCl at 1.0 atm pressure and 273 K . Which of the following statements is incorrect?

[Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{Ca}-40, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5, \mathrm{H}-1$ ]
options:
The limiting reagent is calcium metal.

33.3 g of $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ is produced.

7.84 L of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas is evolved.

0.35 mol of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas is evolved.
questions.solution:
Balanced reaction (NCERT method):

$$\mathrm{Ca} + 2\mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl_2} + \mathrm{H_2}$$

Step 1: Moles of calcium

Given mass of Ca $=14.0\ \mathrm{g}$, molar mass of Ca $=40\ \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$

$$n(\mathrm{Ca})=\frac{14.0}{40}=0.35\ \mathrm{mol}$$

Since $\mathrm{HCl}$ is in excess, the limiting reagent is Ca.

So Option A is correct.

Step 2: Amount of products formed (from stoichiometry)

From the balanced equation:

  • $1\ \mathrm{mol\ Ca} \rightarrow 1\ \mathrm{mol\ CaCl_2}$

  • $1\ \mathrm{mol\ Ca} \rightarrow 1\ \mathrm{mol\ H_2}$

Hence,

$$n(\mathrm{CaCl_2})=0.35\ \mathrm{mol}$$

$$n(\mathrm{H_2})=0.35\ \mathrm{mol}$$

So Option D is correct.

Step 3: Volume of $\mathrm{H_2}$ at $1\ \mathrm{atm}$ and $273\ \mathrm{K}$ (STP)

At STP, $1\ \mathrm{mol}$ gas occupies $22.4\ \mathrm{L}$.

$$V(\mathrm{H_2})=0.35\times 22.4=7.84\ \mathrm{L}$$

So Option C is correct.

Step 4: Mass of $\mathrm{CaCl_2}$

Molar mass of $\mathrm{CaCl_2}$:

$$=40 + 2(35.5)=40+71=111\ \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$$

Mass produced:

$$m(\mathrm{CaCl_2})=0.35\times 111=38.85\ \mathrm{g}$$

But Option B says $33.3\ \mathrm{g}$, which is wrong.

Incorrect statement: Option B.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Evening Shift
questions.question:
Identify the correct statements :

A. Hydrated salts can be used as primary standard.

B. Primary standard should not undergo any reaction with air.

C. Reactions of primary standard with another substance should be instantaneous and stoichiometric.

D. Primary standard should not be soluble in water.

E. Primary standard should have low relative molar mass.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
D and E Only

A, B and E Only

A, B, C and E Only

A, B and C Only
questions.solution:
Primary standard (as per NCERT idea) should be a substance which is pure, stable in air, non-hygroscopic, readily soluble in water, and reacts completely with the titrant in a stoichiometric way.

Now check each statement:

A. Hydrated salts can be used as primary standard.

Some hydrated salts are sufficiently stable and can be used (example often cited: Mohr’s salt). So this can be correct.

B. Primary standard should not undergo any reaction with air.

Correct. It should not react with $O_2$, $CO_2$ or moisture of air.

C. Reactions of primary standard with another substance should be instantaneous and stoichiometric.

Correct. The reaction should be fast and have a definite stoichiometry so the end point is sharp.

D. Primary standard should not be soluble in water.

Incorrect. It should be readily soluble so that a standard solution can be prepared.

E. Primary standard should have low relative molar mass.

Incorrect. It should preferably have high molar mass to minimise weighing error.

So, correct statements are A, B and C only.

Correct option: Option D (A, B and C Only).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Evening Shift
questions.question:
$\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AB}_2$

36.0 g of 'A' (Molar mass : $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) and 56.0 g of ' B ' (Molar mass : $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) are allowed to react. Which of the following statements are correct ?

A. 'A' is the limiting reagent.

B. $77.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{AB}_2$ is formed.

C. Molar mass of $\mathrm{AB}_2$ is $140 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

D. $15.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of A is left unreacted after the completion of reaction.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
A and C Only

A and B Only

C and D Only

B and D Only
questions.solution:
Balanced reaction:

$$A + 2B \longrightarrow AB_2$$

Step 1: Calculate moles of reactants

For $A$:

$$n_A=\frac{36.0}{60}=0.60\ \text{mol}$$

For $B$:

$$n_B=\frac{56.0}{80}=0.70\ \text{mol}$$

Step 2: Find the limiting reagent (NCERT method: compare required moles)

For $0.60$ mol of $A$, required moles of $B$:

$$n_B(\text{required})=2 \times 0.60=1.20\ \text{mol}$$

But available $B = 0.70$ mol, which is less than $1.20$ mol.

So, $B$ is the limiting reagent.

Hence statement A is false.

Step 3: Moles and mass of product formed

From the reaction, $2$ mol $B$ gives $1$ mol $AB_2$.

So moles of $AB_2$ formed:

$$n_{AB_2}=\frac{0.70}{2}=0.35\ \text{mol}$$

Molar mass of $AB_2$:

$$M_{AB_2}=60 + 2(80)=220\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

So statement C is false.

Mass of $AB_2$ formed:

$$m_{AB_2}=0.35 \times 220=77.0\ \text{g}$$

So statement B is true.

Step 4: Unreacted $A$

Moles of $A$ consumed = moles of $AB_2$ formed (ratio $1:1$):

$$n_A(\text{consumed})=0.35\ \text{mol}$$

Mass of $A$ consumed:

$$m_A(\text{consumed})=0.35 \times 60=21.0\ \text{g}$$

Unreacted $A$:

$$m_A(\text{left})=36.0-21.0=15.0\ \text{g}$$

So statement D is true.

Correct statements: B and D only

✅ Option D

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Morning Shift
questions.question:
In the reaction,

$$ 2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{~s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) $$
options: $11.2 \mathrm{~L} \,\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of HCl consumed.
$33.6 \mathrm{~L} \,\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ is produced regardless of temperature and pressure for every mole of Al that reacts.
$67.2 \mathrm{~L} \,\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of Al that reacts.
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+ 3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g}) \\ & \text { From the balanced equation, }6\text{ moles of }\mathrm{HCl}\text{ give }3\text{ moles of }\mathrm{H}_2. \\ & \text { So, moles of }\mathrm{H}_2\text{ produced } \\ & =\frac{3}{6}\times \text{ moles of }\mathrm{HCl}\text{ used } \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\times \text{ moles of }\mathrm{HCl}\text{ used } \\ & \text { Also, }2\text{ moles of }\mathrm{Al}\text{ give }3\text{ moles of }\mathrm{H}_2. \\ & \text { So, moles of }\mathrm{H}_2\text{ produced } \\ & =\frac{3}{2}\times \text{ moles of }\mathrm{Al}\text{ used } \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Morning Shift
questions.question:
$x $ $\mathrm{mg}$ of pure HCl was used to make an aqueous solution. 25.0 mL of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ solution is used when the HCl solution was titrated against it. The numerical value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1}$. (Nearest integer)

Given : Molar mass of HCl and $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ are 36.5 and $171.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
questions.solution:
Balanced equation:

$$\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2 + 2HCl \rightarrow BaCl_2 + 2H_2O}$$

Moles of $\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}$ used:

$$n_{\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}}=MV=0.1\times 25.0\times 10^{-3}=2.5\times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}$$

From the equation, $1$ mol $\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}$ reacts with $2$ mol $\mathrm{HCl}$, so

$$n_{\mathrm{HCl}}=2\times 2.5\times 10^{-3}=5.0\times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}$$

Mass of $\mathrm{HCl}$:

$$m_{\mathrm{HCl}}=n\times M=5.0\times 10^{-3}\times 36.5=0.1825\ \text{g}$$

$$0.1825\ \text{g}=182.5\ \text{mg}$$

So $x=182.5\ \text{mg}=1825\times 10^{-1}\ \text{mg}$.

Nearest integer $= \boxed{1825}$.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift
questions.question:
0.25 g of an organic compound "A" containing carbon, hydrogen and oxygen was analysed using the combustion method. There was an increase in mass of $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ tube and potash tube at the end of the experiment. The amount was found to be 0.15 g and 0.1837 g , respectively. The percentage of oxygen in compound A is

$\_\_\_\_$ %. (Nearest integer)

(Given: molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{O}: 16$ )
questions.solution:
From combustion:

  • Increase in mass of CaCl₂ tube = mass of H₂O formed

    $ m(\text{H}_2\text{O})=0.15\text{ g},\quad n(\text{H}_2\text{O})=\frac{0.15}{18}=0.008333\text{ mol} $

    Moles of H atoms:

    $ n(\text{H})=2n(\text{H}_2\text{O})=0.016667\text{ mol} $

    Mass of H:

    $ m(\text{H})=0.016667\times 1=0.016667\text{ g} $

  • Increase in mass of potash tube = mass of CO₂ formed

    $ m(\text{CO}_2)=0.1837\text{ g},\quad n(\text{CO}_2)=\frac{0.1837}{44}=0.004175\text{ mol} $

    Moles of C atoms = moles of CO₂:

    $ n(\text{C})=0.004175\text{ mol} $

    Mass of C:

    $ m(\text{C})=0.004175\times 12=0.05010\text{ g} $

Sample mass = 0.25 g, so mass of oxygen in the compound:

$ m(\text{O}) = 0.25 - (0.016667+0.05010)=0.18323\text{ g} $

Percentage of oxygen:

$ \% \text{O}=\frac{0.18323}{0.25}\times 100=73.29\%\approx \boxed{73\%} $

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
questions.question:
For the given reaction;

CaCO3 + 2HCl → CaCl2 + H2O + CO2

If 90 g CaCO3 is added to 300 mL of HCl which contains 38.55% HCl by mass and has density 1.13 g mL−1, then which of the following option is correct?

Given molar mass of H, Cl, Ca and O are 1, 35.5, 40 and 16 g mol−1 respectively.
questions.solution:
Density of HCl solution $(\mathrm{d})=1.13 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{ml}$

$$ \mathrm{V}=300 \mathrm{ml} $$

Wt. of HCl solution $= \mathrm{density} \times \mathrm{volume}=1.13 \times 300=339 \mathrm{~g}$

Wt. of $\mathrm{HCl}=339 \times \frac{38.55}{100}=130.68 \mathrm{~g}$ (LR)

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CaCO}_3+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_2 \\ & \text{Molar mass of } \mathrm{CaCO}_3 =40+12+(3 \times 16)=100 \mathrm{~g~mol^{-1}} \\ & \text{So, moles of } \mathrm{CaCO}_3=\frac{90}{100}=0.90 \text { mole } \\ & \text{Molar mass of } \mathrm{HCl}=1+35.5=36.5 \mathrm{~g~mol^{-1}} \\ & \text{So, moles of } \mathrm{HCl}=\frac{130.68}{36.5}=3.58 \text { mole } \\ & \text{From the equation, } 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \text{ needs } 2 \text{ mol } \mathrm{HCl} \\ & \text{So, } 0.90 \text{ mol } \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \text{ needs } 2 \times 0.90=1.80 \text{ mol } \mathrm{HCl} \end{aligned} $$

Since available HCl $=3.58$ mol and required HCl $=1.80$ mol, HCl is in excess and $\mathrm{CaCO}_3$ is the limiting reagent (LR).

Moles of HCl remained $=3.58-1.80=1.78$ mole.

Mass of HCl remained $=1.78 \times 36.5=64.97 \mathrm{~g}$.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
questions.question:
The ratio of mass percentage (w/w) of C : H in a hydrocarbon is 12 : 1. It has two carbon atoms. The weight (in g) of CO2(g) formed when 3.38 g of this hydrocarbon is completely burnt in oxygen is :

(Given : Molar mass in g mol–1 C : 12, H : 1, O : 16)
questions.solution:
Let the hydrocarbon be $C_2H_x$ since it has two carbon atoms.

Given, the mass percentage ratio of $C:H$ is $12:1$.

So, in the hydrocarbon,

  • mass of carbon $= 2 \times 12 = 24$

  • mass of hydrogen $= x \times 1 = x$

Now,

$$ 24 : x = 12 : 1 $$

So,

$$ \frac{24}{x} = \frac{12}{1} $$

$$ 24 = 12x $$

$$ x = 2 $$

Hence, the hydrocarbon is

$$ C_2H_2 $$

Its molar mass is

$$ (2 \times 12) + (2 \times 1) = 24 + 2 = 26 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

Number of moles of $C_2H_2$ in $3.38$ g:

$$ \text{moles of } C_2H_2 = \frac{3.38}{26} = 0.13 \text{ mol} $$

Complete combustion of acetylene:

$$ 2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O $$

From the equation,

$$ 1 \text{ mol } C_2H_2 \rightarrow 2 \text{ mol } CO_2 $$

Therefore,

$$ 0.13 \text{ mol } C_2H_2 \rightarrow 0.26 \text{ mol } CO_2 $$

Molar mass of $CO_2$:

$$ 12 + (2 \times 16) = 44 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

Mass of $CO_2$ formed:

$$ 0.26 \times 44 = 11.44 \text{ g} $$

So, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{11.44 \text{ g}} $$

Option B.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
questions.question:
The mass of iron converted into Fe3O4 by the action of 18 g of steam is:

(Given: Molar mass of H, O and Fe are 1, 16 and 56 g mol−1 respectively)

Assume iron is present in excess:
questions.solution:
The reaction of iron with steam is:

$$ 3Fe + 4H_2O \rightarrow Fe_3O_4 + 4H_2 $$

We are given:

$$ \text{Mass of steam} = 18 \text{ g} $$

Molar mass of water:

$$ H_2O = 2(1) + 16 = 18 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

So, moles of steam are:

$$ \text{Moles of } H_2O = \frac{18}{18} = 1 \text{ mol} $$

From the balanced equation:

$$ 4 \text{ mol } H_2O \text{ react with } 3 \text{ mol } Fe $$

Therefore, for $1$ mol $H_2O$:

$$ \text{Moles of Fe required} = \frac{3}{4} $$

Now, molar mass of iron is:

$$ 56 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

So, mass of iron reacted:

$$ \text{Mass of Fe} = \frac{3}{4} \times 56 = 42 \text{ g} $$

Hence, the mass of iron converted into $Fe_3O_4$ is:

$$ \boxed{42 \text{ g}} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
The correct order of total number of atoms present in

(A) 2 moles of cyclohexane

(B) 684 g of sucrose

(C) 90.8 L of dihydrogen at STP

is :
questions.solution:
Let us find the total number of atoms in each case.

We only need to compare, so first calculate the number of moles of atoms in each sample.

(A) 2 moles of cyclohexane

Cyclohexane has formula:

$$ \mathrm{C_6H_{12}} $$

So, one molecule of cyclohexane contains:

$$ 6 + 12 = 18 \text{ atoms} $$

Therefore, in $2$ moles of cyclohexane, moles of atoms are:

$$ 2 \times 18 = 36 \text{ moles of atoms} $$

(B) 684 g of sucrose

Sucrose has formula:

$$ \mathrm{C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}} $$

Total atoms in one molecule:

$$ 12 + 22 + 11 = 45 $$

Molar mass of sucrose:

$$ 12 \times 12 + 22 \times 1 + 11 \times 16 $$

$$ = 144 + 22 + 176 = 342 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

Given mass $= 684$ g, so moles of sucrose:

$$ \frac{684}{342} = 2 \text{ mol} $$

Hence, moles of atoms:

$$ 2 \times 45 = 90 \text{ moles of atoms} $$

(C) 90.8 L of dihydrogen at STP

At STP, $1$ mole of any gas occupies $22.4$ L.

So moles of $\mathrm{H_2}$:

$$ \frac{90.8}{22.4} \approx 4.05 \text{ mol} $$

Each molecule of $\mathrm{H_2}$ has $2$ atoms.

Therefore, moles of atoms:

$$ 4.05 \times 2 = 8.10 \text{ moles of atoms} $$

Comparison

  • (A) $= 36$ moles of atoms

  • (B) $= 90$ moles of atoms

  • (C) $= 8.10$ moles of atoms

So the correct order is:

$$ \mathrm{B} > \mathrm{A} > \mathrm{C} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: Number of moles and number of molecules in 1.4187 L of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ at STP respectively are
questions.solution:
At STP, according to Avogadro’s law, $1$ mole of any gas occupies $22.4 \, \text{L}$.

So, number of moles of $\mathrm{SO_2}$ is

$$ n=\frac{V}{22.4}=\frac{1.4187}{22.4} $$

$$ n=0.0633 \text{ mol} $$

Now, number of molecules

$$ = n \times N_A $$

where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$

So,

$$ \text{Number of molecules} = 0.0633 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} $$

$$ = 3.812 \times 10^{22} $$

Hence, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{0.0633 \text{ mol ; } 3.812 \times 10^{22} \text{ molecules}} $$

So, Option B is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
What volume of hydrogen gas at STP would be liberated by action of 50 mL of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ of $50 \%$ purity (density $=1.3 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ ) on 20 g of zinc?

Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Zn}$ are 1, 16, 32, $65 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
questions.solution:
The reaction is:

$$ \mathrm{Zn} + \mathrm{H_2SO_4} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO_4} + \mathrm{H_2} $$

From the balanced equation,

$$ 1 \text{ mol Zn } \text{ reacts with } 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{H_2SO_4} \text{ and gives } 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{H_2} $$

Now let us find the number of moles of each reactant.

1. Amount of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ present

Volume of acid solution $= 50 \, \mathrm{mL}$

Density $= 1.3 \, \mathrm{g\,mL^{-1}}$

So, mass of acid solution is

$$ \text{Mass} = \text{Volume} \times \text{Density} = 50 \times 1.3 = 65 \, \mathrm{g} $$

Purity $= 50\%$

Hence, mass of pure $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ is

$$ \frac{50}{100} \times 65 = 32.5 \, \mathrm{g} $$

Molar mass of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$:

$$ 2(1) + 32 + 4(16) = 98 \, \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}} $$

So, moles of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ are

$$ \frac{32.5}{98} = 0.3316 \text{ mol} $$

2. Amount of zinc

Mass of Zn $= 20 \, \mathrm{g}$

Molar mass of Zn $= 65 \, \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$

So, moles of Zn are

$$ \frac{20}{65} = 0.3077 \text{ mol} $$

3. Limiting reagent

Since the reaction ratio is $1:1$:

  • $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ available $= 0.3316$ mol

  • Zn available $= 0.3077$ mol

Zn is smaller in amount, so zinc is the limiting reagent.

Therefore, moles of $\mathrm{H_2}$ formed are

$$ 0.3077 \text{ mol} $$

4. Volume of hydrogen at STP

At STP, $1$ mol gas occupies $22.4 \, \mathrm{L}$.

So, volume of $\mathrm{H_2}$ is

$$ 0.3077 \times 22.4 = 6.892 \, \mathrm{L} $$

$$ \boxed{6.892 \, \mathrm{L}} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C}} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating 2.76 g of silver carbonate?

(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ag are 12, 16 and $108 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
questions.solution:
$$ \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3}(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\mathrm{Ag}(s) + \mathrm{CO_2}(g) + \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2}(g) $$

From the chemical equation, we can see that carbon dioxide ($ \mathrm{CO_2} $) and oxygen ($ \mathrm{O_2} $) are gases and will easily escape. The only solid residue left behind is silver ($ \mathrm{Ag} $).

Next, let us calculate the molar mass of silver carbonate ($ \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3} $) by adding the molar masses of its constituent elements:

$$ \text{Molar mass of } \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3} = (2 \times 108) + 12 + (3 \times 16) $$

$$ \text{Molar mass of } \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3} = 216 + 12 + 48 = 276 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

Now, we can find the number of moles of silver carbonate provided in the question. The given mass is $ 2.76 \mathrm{~g} $.

$$ \text{Number of moles} = \frac{\text{Given mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} $$

$$ \text{Moles of } \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3} = \frac{2.76 \mathrm{~g}}{276 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} = 0.01 \mathrm{~mol} $$

Using the stoichiometry from our balanced chemical equation, we know that heating $ 1 \mathrm{~mol} $ of $ \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3} $ yields $ 2 \mathrm{~mol} $ of solid $ \mathrm{Ag} $ residue.

Therefore, if we decomposition $ 0.01 \mathrm{~mol} $ of $ \mathrm{Ag_2CO_3} $, the number of moles of silver residue produced will be:

$$ \text{Moles of residue } (\mathrm{Ag}) = 2 \times 0.01 \mathrm{~mol} = 0.02 \mathrm{~mol} $$

Finally, we convert these moles of silver back to mass in grams. We know the given molar mass of $ \mathrm{Ag} $ is $ 108 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $.

$$ \text{Mass of } \mathrm{Ag} = \text{Moles of } \mathrm{Ag} \times \text{Molar mass of } \mathrm{Ag} $$

$$ \text{Mass of } \mathrm{Ag} = 0.02 \mathrm{~mol} \times 108 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} = 2.16 \mathrm{~g} $$

The mass of the solid residue obtained is $ 2.16 \mathrm{~g} $.

Correct Option: Option B

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
What is the mole fraction of water in $10 \%$ by weight $(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ of aqueous urea solution?

[Given: Molar mass of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{C}$ and N are $1,16,12$ and $14 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.]
questions.solution:
For a $10\%$ by weight $(w/w)$ aqueous urea solution, let us take $100 \, \text{g}$ of solution.

Then:

  • Mass of urea $= 10 \, \text{g}$

  • Mass of water $= 90 \, \text{g}$

Now find the molar masses.

Urea has formula $NH_2CONH_2$ or $CO(NH_2)_2$

$$ M(\text{urea}) = 12 + 16 + 2(14) + 4(1) = 60 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Moles of urea:

$$ n_{\text{urea}} = \frac{10}{60} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167 $$

Molar mass of water:

$$ M(\text{water}) = 2(1) + 16 = 18 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Moles of water:

$$ n_{\text{water}} = \frac{90}{18} = 5 $$

Now, mole fraction of water is

$$ X_{\text{water}} = \frac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{water}} + n_{\text{urea}}} $$

$$ X_{\text{water}} = \frac{5}{5 + \frac{1}{6}} = \frac{5}{\frac{31}{6}} = \frac{30}{31} $$

$$ X_{\text{water}} \approx 0.967 $$

So, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{0.967} $$

Option D

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
questions.question:
Which of the following contain the same number of atoms?

(Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{He}, \mathrm{O}$ and S are 1, 4, 16 and 32 respectively)

A. 2 g of $\mathrm{O}_2$ gas

B. 4 g of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ gas

C. 1400 mL of $\mathrm{O}_2$ at STP

D. $0.05 \mathrm{~L}$ of He at STP

E. $ 0.0625 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
A and B only

B and C only

C and D only

A, C and E only
questions.solution:
$$ \text{Total number of atoms} = (\text{Number of moles}) \times N_A \times (\text{Atomicity}) $$

Let's calculate this for each option.

A. 2 g of $\mathrm{O}_2$ gas

  1. Molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_2$: The atomic mass of O is 16 u. So, the molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_2$ is $2 \times 16 = 32 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}$.

  2. Number of moles ($n$):

    $$ n = \frac{\text{Given mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} = \frac{2 \ \text{g}}{32 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = \frac{1}{16} \ \text{mol} $$

  3. Atomicity of $\mathrm{O}_2$: One molecule of $\mathrm{O}_2$ contains 2 oxygen atoms. So, the atomicity is 2.

  4. Total number of atoms:

    $$ \text{Atoms} = n \times N_A \times \text{Atomicity} = \frac{1}{16} \times N_A \times 2 = \frac{2}{16} N_A = \frac{1}{8} N_A $$

B. 4 g of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ gas

  1. Molar mass of $\mathrm{SO}_2$: Atomic mass of S is 32 u and O is 16 u. So, the molar mass of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ is $32 + (2 \times 16) = 32 + 32 = 64 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}$.

  2. Number of moles ($n$):

    $$ n = \frac{\text{Given mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} = \frac{4 \ \text{g}}{64 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = \frac{1}{16} \ \text{mol} $$

  3. Atomicity of $\mathrm{SO}_2$: One molecule of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ contains 1 sulfur atom and 2 oxygen atoms, making a total of $1+2=3$ atoms. The atomicity is 3.

  4. Total number of atoms:

    $$ \text{Atoms} = n \times N_A \times \text{Atomicity} = \frac{1}{16} \times N_A \times 3 = \frac{3}{16} N_A $$

C. 1400 mL of $\mathrm{O}_2$ at STP

  1. Molar volume at STP: We know that 1 mole of any gas at STP occupies 22400 mL (or 22.4 L).

  2. Number of moles ($n$):

    $$ n = \frac{\text{Given volume}}{\text{Molar volume at STP}} = \frac{1400 \ \text{mL}}{22400 \ \text{mL mol}^{-1}} = \frac{14}{224} = \frac{1}{16} \ \text{mol} $$

  3. Atomicity of $\mathrm{O}_2$: As we found earlier, the atomicity is 2.

  4. Total number of atoms:

    $$ \text{Atoms} = n \times N_A \times \text{Atomicity} = \frac{1}{16} \times N_A \times 2 = \frac{2}{16} N_A = \frac{1}{8} N_A $$

D. 0.05 L of He at STP

  1. Molar volume at STP: 1 mole of any gas at STP occupies 22.4 L.

  2. Number of moles ($n$):

    $$ n = \frac{\text{Given volume}}{\text{Molar volume at STP}} = \frac{0.05 \ \text{L}}{22.4 \ \text{L mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0022 \ \text{mol} $$

  3. Atomicity of He: Helium (He) is a noble gas and exists as single atoms. So, its atomicity is 1.

  4. Total number of atoms:

    $$ \text{Atoms} = n \times N_A \times \text{Atomicity} = \frac{0.05}{22.4} \times N_A \times 1 = \frac{0.05}{22.4} N_A $$

E. 0.0625 mol of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas

  1. Number of moles ($n$): This is given directly as $0.0625 \ \text{mol}$. Let's convert this decimal to a fraction to make comparison easier: $0.0625 = \frac{625}{10000} = \frac{1}{16}$. So, $n = \frac{1}{16} \ \text{mol}$.

  2. Atomicity of $\mathrm{H}_2$: One molecule of $\mathrm{H}_2$ contains 2 hydrogen atoms. The atomicity is 2.

  3. Total number of atoms:

    $$ \text{Atoms} = n \times N_A \times \text{Atomicity} = \frac{1}{16} \times N_A \times 2 = \frac{2}{16} N_A = \frac{1}{8} N_A $$

Conclusion

Let's summarise the total number of atoms we calculated for each option:

  • A: $\frac{1}{8} N_A$ atoms

  • B: $\frac{3}{16} N_A$ atoms

  • C: $\frac{1}{8} N_A$ atoms

  • D: $\frac{0.05}{22.4} N_A$ atoms

  • E: $\frac{1}{8} N_A$ atoms

By comparing these results, we can clearly see that options A, C, and E contain the same number of atoms.

Therefore, the correct choice is the one that lists A, C, and E.

The correct answer from the options given below is Option D.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
An oxide of iron contains $69.9 \%$ iron, its empirical formula, is :

(Given : Molar mass of Fe and O are 56 and $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.)
questions.solution:
Let the oxide of iron contain 100 g of the compound.

Then,

Mass of Fe = 69.9 g

Mass of O = 100 − 69.9 = 30.1 g

Calculate moles of each element

$ \text{Moles of Fe} = \frac{69.9}{56} = 1.248 $

$ \text{Moles of O} = \frac{30.1}{16} = 1.881 $

Find the simplest whole-number ratio

Divide each by the smaller number (1.248):

$ \text{Ratio of Fe:O} = \frac{1.248}{1.248} : \frac{1.881}{1.248} = 1 : 1.51 $

This ratio is approximately 1 : 1.5.

To remove the fraction, multiply both by 2 : $ \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 $

In $\mathrm{Fe_2O_3}$,

Mass of Fe = $2 \times 56 = 112$ g

Mass of O = $3 \times 16 = 48$ g

Total mass = 160 g

Percentage of Fe = $\dfrac{112}{160} \times 100 = 70\%$ (approximately 69.9%)

Therefore, the empirical formula of the oxide is

$ \boxed{\mathrm{Fe_2O_3}} $

Correct Option: B.

questions.question:
$$ \text { Match List - I with List - II. } $$

List - I
Mass of substance		 List - II
Number of atoms
A. 1.8 mg water I. $$
2 \times 10^{-4} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}
$$
B. 9.8 mg sulphuric acid II. $$
1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}
$$
C. 1.8 mg carbon III. $$
3 \times 10^{-4} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}
$$
D. 5.85 mg salt $(\mathrm{NaCl})$ IV. $$
7 \times 10^{-4} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}
$$

$$ \text { Choose the correct answer from the options given below : } $$
options:
A-IV, B-III, C-I, D-II

A-III, B-II, C-IV, D-I

A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I

A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II
questions.solution:
We have the following masses:

  • A: 1.8 mg H₂O

  • B: 9.8 mg H₂SO₄

  • C: 1.8 mg C

  • D: 5.85 mg NaCl

Since $ 1 \, \text{mg} = 10^{-3} \, \text{g} $:

$ \begin{aligned} \text{A: } & 1.8 \, \text{mg} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{g} \\ \text{B: } & 9.8 \, \text{mg} = 9.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{g} \\ \text{C: } & 1.8 \, \text{mg} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{g} \\ \text{D: } & 5.85 \, \text{mg} = 5.85 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{g} \end{aligned} $

Number of atoms $ = (\text{number of moles of molecules}) \times (\text{atoms per molecule}) \times N_A $

i.e.

$ \text{Number of atoms} = \frac{\text{Given mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} \times (\text{atoms per molecule}) \times N_A $

(A) Water (H₂O)

Molar mass of H₂O = 18 g/mol

Atoms per molecule = 3 (2 H + 1 O)

$ \text{Moles of H₂O} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{18} = 1 \times 10^{-4} $

$ \text{Number of atoms} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 N_A = 3 \times 10^{-4} N_A $

So, A → III

(B) Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)

Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol

Atoms per molecule = 7 (2 H + 1 S + 4 O)

$ \text{Moles of H₂SO₄} = \frac{9.8 \times 10^{-3}}{98} = 1 \times 10^{-4} $

$ \text{Number of atoms} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 7 N_A = 7 \times 10^{-4} N_A $

So, B → IV

(C) Carbon (C)

Molar mass of carbon = 12 g/mol

Each atom = 1 atom per molecule

$ \text{Moles of C} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} $

$ \text{Number of atoms} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} N_A $

So, C → II

(D) Sodium chloride (NaCl)

Molar mass = 58.5 g/mol

Atoms per formula unit = 2 (Na + Cl)

$ \text{Moles of NaCl} = \frac{5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{58.5} = 1 \times 10^{-4} $

$ \text{Number of atoms} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 N_A = 2 \times 10^{-4} N_A $

So, D → I

$ \boxed{ A - III, \quad B - IV, \quad C - II, \quad D - I } $

Option C

questions.question:
A random variable X takes values 0, 1, 2, 3 with probabilities $\frac{2a+1}{30}$, $\frac{8a-1}{30}$, $\frac{4a+1}{30}$, $b$ respectively, where $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$.

Let $\mu$ and $\sigma$ respectively be the mean and standard deviation of $X$ such that $\sigma^2 + \mu^2 = 2$.

Then $\frac{a}{b}$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{X} & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ \hline p(n) & \frac{2 a+1}{30} & \frac{8 a-1}{30} & \frac{4 a+1}{30} & \mathrm{~b} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mu=\Sigma \times p(n)=0+\frac{8 a-1}{30}+2 \times \frac{4 a+1}{30}+3 b=\frac{16 a+90 b+1}{30} \\ & \sigma^2=\Sigma x^2 p(n)-\mu^2 \Rightarrow \sigma^2+\mu^2=\Sigma x^2 p(n)=2 \\ & \frac{8 a-1}{30}+\frac{4(4 a+1)}{30}+9 b=2 \\ & 8 a+90 b-19=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots(i) \\ & \Sigma p(n)=1 \Rightarrow 14 a+1+30 b=30 \\ & 14 a+30 b-29=0 \ldots \ldots . .(i i) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { From (i) and (ii) }\\ &a=2 \quad b=\frac{1}{30}=\frac{a}{b}=60 \end{aligned} $$

questions.question: If the mean deviation about the median of the numbers $\mathrm{k}, 2 \mathrm{k}, 3 \mathrm{k}, \ldots ., 1000 \mathrm{k}$ is 500 , then $\mathrm{k}^2$ is equal to :
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \because \text { median }=\frac{1001 k}{2}=X_M \\ & \therefore \text { mean deviation about median }=\frac{\sum\left|X_i-X_M\right|}{n} \\ & =\frac{2\left(\frac{k}{2}+\frac{3 k}{2}+\frac{5 k}{2}+\ldots . . .500 \text { terms }\right)}{1000}=\frac{2 . \frac{k}{2}(500)^2}{1000}=\frac{500 k}{2}=500(\text { given }) \\ & \therefore k=2 \\ & \therefore k^2=4 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Evening Shift
questions.question:
If the mean and the variance of the data

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Class } & 4-8 & 8-12 & 12-16 & 16-20 \\ \hline \text { Frequency } & 3 & \lambda & 4 & 7 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

are $\mu$ and 19 respectively, then the value of $\lambda+\mu$ is :
questions.solution:
Class fi xi fi·xi fi·xi2
4–8 3 6 18 108
8–12 λ 10 10λ 100λ
12–16 4 14 56 784
16–20 7 18 126 2268
Total 14 + $$\lambda $$ 200 + 10$$\lambda $$ 3160 + 100$$\lambda $$

$\begin{aligned} & \sum f_i=14+\lambda \\ & \sum f_i x_i=18+10 \lambda+56+126=200+10 \lambda \\ & \text { Mean } \mu=\frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}=\frac{200+10 \lambda}{14+\lambda} \\ & \sum f_i x_i^2=3\left(6^2\right)+\lambda\left(10^2\right)+4\left(14^2\right)+7\left(18^2\right)=108+100 \lambda+784+2268=3160+100 \lambda\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Variance }=\frac{\sum f_i x_i^2}{\sum f_i}-\mu^2=19 \\ & 19=\frac{3160+100 \lambda}{14+\lambda}-\left(\frac{200+10 \lambda}{14+\lambda}\right)^2 \\ & 19(14+\lambda)^2=(3160+100 \lambda)(14+\lambda)-(200+10 \lambda)^2 \\ & 19\left(196+28 \lambda+\lambda^2\right)=44240+4560 \lambda+100 \lambda^2-40000-4000 \lambda-100 \lambda^2 \\ & 3724+532 \lambda+19 \lambda^2=4240+560 \lambda \\ & 19 \lambda^2-28 \lambda-516=0\end{aligned}$

Solving for $\lambda$ using the quadratic formula:

$$ \lambda=\frac{28 \pm \sqrt{28^2-4(19)(-516)}}{38}=\frac{28 \pm 200}{38} $$

Taking the positive value, $\lambda=\frac{228}{38}=6$

Finding the mean $\mu$ :

$$ \mu=\frac{200+10(6)}{14+6}=\frac{260}{20}=13 $$

The value of $\lambda+\mu$ is:

$$ 6+13=19 $$

questions.question:
Let the mean and variance of 8 numbers $-10,-7,-1, x, y, 9,2,16$ be $\frac{7}{2}$ and $\frac{293}{4}$, respectively.

Then the mean of 4 numbers $x, y, x+y+1,|x-y|$ is :
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=\frac{7}{2} \\ & \frac{-10-7-1+x+y+16+2+9}{8}=\frac{7}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+9=28 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=19 \\ & \text { Variance }=\frac{293}{4} \\ & \frac{100+49+1+x^2+y^2+256+4+81}{8}-\frac{49}{4}=\frac{293}{4} \\ & \frac{491+x^2+y^2}{8}=\frac{342}{4} \\ & x^2+y^2=193 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(x, y)=(7,12) \text { or }(12,7) \\ & \text { Mean }=\frac{x+y+x+y+1+(x-y)}{4} \\ & =\frac{19+20+5}{4} \\ & =11 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift
questions.question: Let $\mathrm{X}=\{x \in \mathrm{~N}: 1 \leq x \leq 19\}$ and for some $a, b \in \mathbb{R}, \mathrm{Y}=\{a x+b: x \in \mathrm{X}\}$. If the mean and variance of the elements of Y are 30 and 750 , respectively, then the sum of all possible values of $b$ is
questions.solution:
$$ Y=\{a x+b: x \in X\} . \text { and } X=\{x \in \mathbb{N}: 1 \leq x \leq 19\} $$

mean for $Y$ is 30.

$$\therefore $$ $(\bar{Y}=30)$

$\bar{Y}=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} Y_i}{19}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} a x_i+b}{19}=30$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} a x_i+\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} b=19 \times 30$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a(1+2+\cdots+19)+19 b=19 \times 30$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a \times \frac{19 \times 20}{2}+19 b=570$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ 10 a+b=30 \Rightarrow a=\frac{30-b}{10} $$ .......(1)

Variance for $Y$ is 750

$$ \sigma^2=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} Y_i^2}{19}-(\bar{Y})^2=750 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19}\left(a x_i+b\right)^2}{19}-(30)^2=750$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} a^2 x_i^2+b^2+2 a x_i=(750+900) 19$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a^2 \sum\limits_{i=1}^{19}\left(x_i^2+b^2+2 a \sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} x_i\right)=1650 \times 19$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a^2\left(1^2+2^2+\cdots+19^2\right)+\sum\limits_{i=1}^{19} b^2+2 a b(1+2+\cdots+19)=1650 \times 19$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a^2 \cdot \frac{19 \times 20 \times 39}{6}+19 b^2+\frac{2 a b \times 19 \times 20}{2}=1650 \times 19$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ 130 a^2+b^2+20 a b=1650 $$ ......(2)

Substitute value of $a$ from (1) in equation (2)

$$ 130\left(\frac{30-b}{10}\right)^2+b^2+20 b\left(\frac{30-b}{10}\right)=1650 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{13}{10}\left(900+b^2-60 b\right)+b^2+60 b-2 b^2=1650$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1170+1.3 b^2-78 b+10 b^2+600 b-20 b^2=16500$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $3 b^2-180 b-4800=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $b^2-60 b-1600=0$

Let roots of quadratic equation is $b_1, b_2$

So sum of possible values of $b=$ sum of roots

$$ b_1+b_2=\frac{- \text { coefficient of } b}{\text { coefficient of } b^2}=60 $$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift
questions.question: The mean and variance of a data of 10 observations are 10 and 2 , respectively. If an observations $\alpha$ in this data is replaced by $\beta$, then the mean and variance become 10.1 and 1.99 , respectively. Then $\alpha+\beta$ equals
questions.solution:
Given, total observation $n=10$

Initial mean $\bar{x}_1=10 $

$\Rightarrow \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i}{10}=10$

Sum of 10 observation $\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i=10 \times 10=100$

Initial variance $$\sigma _1^2$$$=2$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2}{n}-\left(\bar{x}_1\right)^2=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2=n\left[2+\left(\bar{x}_1\right)^2\right] =10\left[2+\left(10^2\right)\right]=1020\end{aligned}$

It is given that if $\alpha$ is replace by $\beta$

then, new mean $\left(\bar{x}_2\right)=10.1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \bar{x}_2=\frac{100-\alpha+\beta}{10}=10.1 \\ & \Rightarrow 100-\alpha+\beta=101 \\ & \Rightarrow \beta-\alpha=1......(1) \end{aligned} $$

Also new variance $\sigma_2^2=1.99$ (given)

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\sigma_2^2=\frac{1020-\alpha^2+\beta^2}{10}-(10.1)^2=1.99$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1020-\alpha^2+\beta^2=10[1.99+102.01]$$=10 \times 104=1040$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \beta^2-\alpha^2=20 \\\\ & \Rightarrow (\beta+\alpha)(\beta-\alpha)=20\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$$(\beta+\alpha)(1)=20$ (substitute $\beta-\alpha=1)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\alpha+\beta=20$

questions.question: The mean and variance of 10 observations are 9 and 34.2 , respectively. If 8 of these observations are $2,3,5,10,11,13,15,21$, then the mean deviation about the median of all the 10 observations is
questions.solution:
Let the missing observations be $x$ and $y$.

$$ \text { Mean }(\bar{x})=9: \frac{2+3+5+10+11+13+15+21+x+y}{10}=9 $$

$$ 80+x+y=90 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{1 0} $$

$$ \text { Variance }\left(\sigma^2\right)=34.2 : \sigma^2=\frac{\sum x_i^2}{n}-(\bar{x})^2 $$

$$ \frac{2^2+3^2+5^2+10^2+11^2+13^2+15^2+21^2+x^2+y^2}{10}-9^2=34.2 $$

$$ \frac{4+9+25+100+121+169+225+441+x^2+y^2}{10}-81=34.2 $$

$$ \frac{1094+x^2+y^2}{10}=115.2 $$

$$ 1094+x^2+y^2=1152 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}^2+\mathbf{y}^2=\mathbf{5 8} $$

Solving $x+y=10$ and $x^2+y^2=58$

To solve the system $x+y=10$ and $x^2+y^2=58$ by finding $x-y$ use the formula $x-y=\sqrt{2\left(x^2+y^2\right)-(x+y)^2}$ :

$$ x-y=\sqrt{2\left(x^2+y^2\right)-(x+y)^2} $$

$$ x-y=\sqrt{2(58)-(10)^2}=\sqrt{116-100}=\sqrt{16} $$

$$ x-y=4 $$

Now add these equations $x+y=10 \& x-y=4$

$$ 2 x=14 \Longrightarrow x=7 $$

Put $x=7$ in $x+y=10$

$$ 7+y=10 \Longrightarrow y=3 $$

missing numbers are 3 and 7 .

Find the Median

The 10 observations in ascending order are: $2,3,3,5,7,10,11,13,15,21$.

  • Since $n=10$, the median is the average of the 5th and 6th terms :

    Median $=\frac{7+10}{2}=$ 8.5

Calculate Mean Deviation about Median

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { M.D. }(\text { median })=\frac{\sum \mid x_i-\text { Median } \mid}{n} \\ & = \frac{|2-8.5|+|3-8.5|+|3-8.5|+|5-8.5|+|7-8.5|+|10-8.5|+|11-8.5|+|13-8.5|+|15-8.5|+|21-8.5|}{10} \\ & =\frac{6.5+5.5+5.5+3.5+1.5+1.5+2.5+4.5+6.5+12.5}{10}=\frac{50}{10}=\mathbf{5} \end{aligned} $$

questions.question: For 10 observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{10}$, if $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10}\left(x_i+2\right)^2=180$ and $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10}\left(x_i-1\right)^2=90$, then their standard deviation is :
questions.solution:
$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} (x_i + 2)^2 = 180$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} 4 = 180$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 180 - 40 \quad \ldots (1)$$

Also $$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} (x_i - 1)^2 = 90$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} 1 = 90$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 90 - 10 \quad \ldots (2)$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 140$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 80$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 = 100 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 10$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2}{N} - \left(\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i}{N}\right)^2$$

$$= \frac{100}{10} - \left(\frac{10}{10}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma^2 = 10 - 1 = 9$$

$$\Rightarrow \sigma = 3$$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: Suppose that the mean and median of the non-negative numbers $21,8,17, a, 51,103, b, 13,67,(a>b)$, are 40 and 21 , respectively. If the mean deviation about the median is 26 , then $2 a$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
The given data set has $n = 9$ observations: $21, 8, 17, a, 51, 103, b, 13, 67$.

The mean ($\bar{x}$) of these numbers is given as $40$.

The formula for the mean is:

$$ \text{Mean} = \frac{\text{Sum of all observations}}{n} $$

Substitute the given values into the formula:

$$ 40 = \frac{21 + 8 + 17 + a + 51 + 103 + b + 13 + 67}{9} $$

Multiply both sides by 9:

$$ 360 = 280 + a + b $$

$$ a + b = 360 - 280 $$

$$ a + b = 80 \quad \text{--- (Equation 1)} $$

The median ($M$) of the data is $21$. For $n = 9$ (an odd number), the median is the $\left(\frac{9 + 1}{2}\right)^{\text{th}} = 5^{\text{th}}$ observation when aligned in ascending order.

Let's arrange the known numbers in ascending order:

$8, 13, 17, 21, 51, 67, 103$

Here, there are exactly three numbers less than $21$ ($8, 13, 17$) and three numbers greater than $21$ ($51, 67, 103$).

For $21$ to remain the middle (5th) observation among all 9 numbers, one of the unknown variables ($a$ or $b$) must be less than or equal to $21$, and the other must be greater than or equal to $21$.

Since it is given that $a > b$, we can conclude:

$a \ge 21$ and $b \le 21$

The formula for mean deviation about the median is:

$$ \text{M.D. (M)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - M|}{n} $$

We are given that $\text{M.D. (M)} = 26$.

Substitute the values:

$$ 26 = \frac{|21 - 21| + |8 - 21| + |17 - 21| + |a - 21| + |51 - 21| + |103 - 21| + |b - 21| + |13 - 21| + |67 - 21|}{9} $$

Multiply by 9 and simplify the absolute values for the known numbers:

$$ 234 = 0 + 13 + 4 + |a - 21| + 30 + 82 + |b - 21| + 8 + 46 $$

$$ 234 = 183 + |a - 21| + |b - 21| $$

$$ |a - 21| + |b - 21| = 234 - 183 $$

$$ |a - 21| + |b - 21| = 51 $$

From Step 2, we know that $a \ge 21 \implies |a - 21| = a - 21$ and $b \le 21 \implies |b - 21| = 21 - b$. Replacing these in the equation:

$$ (a - 21) + (21 - b) = 51 $$

$$ a - b = 51 \quad \text{--- (Equation 2)} $$

Now we have a system of two linear equations:

1) $a + b = 80$

2) $a - b = 51$

To find $2a$, simply add Equation 1 and Equation 2:

$$ (a + b) + (a - b) = 80 + 51 $$

$$ 2a = 131 $$

Therefore, the value of $2a$ is $131$.

Option D is the correct answer.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
questions.question: A variable $X$ takes values $0,0,2,6,12,20, \ldots, n(n-1)$ with frequencies ${ }^n C_0,{ }^n C_1,{ }^n C_2,{ }^n C_3,{ }^n C_4,{ }^n C_5, \ldots,{ }^n C_n$, respectively. If the mean of this data is 60 , then its median is :
questions.solution:
The given variable $X$ takes values

$$ 0,0,2,6,12,20,\ldots,n(n-1) $$

with corresponding frequencies

$$ {}^nC_0,{}^nC_1,{}^nC_2,{}^nC_3,{}^nC_4,{}^nC_5,\ldots,{}^nC_n $$

We notice that the values of $X$ follow the pattern

$$ X=r(r-1), \quad r=0,1,2,\ldots,n $$

and the corresponding frequency is

$$ {}^nC_r $$

So this is a discrete frequency distribution with:

  • variate value $x_r=r(r-1)$

  • frequency $f_r={}^nC_r$

Step 1: Use the formula for mean

Mean is given by

$$ \bar X=\frac{\sum f_r x_r}{\sum f_r} $$

So,

$$ \bar X=\frac{\sum_{r=0}^n {}^nC_r \, r(r-1)}{\sum_{r=0}^n {}^nC_r} $$

Given mean $=60$, so

$$ \frac{\sum_{r=0}^n {}^nC_r \, r(r-1)}{\sum_{r=0}^n {}^nC_r}=60 $$

Now use standard binomial identities:

$$ \sum_{r=0}^n {}^nC_r = 2^n $$

and

$$ \sum_{r=0}^n r(r-1)\,{}^nC_r = n(n-1)2^{n-2} $$

Hence,

$$ \bar X=\frac{n(n-1)2^{n-2}}{2^n} =\frac{n(n-1)}{4} $$

Given this is $60$:

$$ \frac{n(n-1)}{4}=60 $$

$$ n(n-1)=240 $$

$$ n^2-n-240=0 $$

$$ (n-16)(n+15)=0 $$

So,

$$ n=16 $$

Step 2: Write the values and frequencies for $n=16$

Now total frequency is

$$ N=\sum_{r=0}^{16} {}^{16}C_r = 2^{16}=65536 $$

To find the median, we need the value of $X$ whose cumulative frequency just exceeds $\dfrac{N}{2}$.

$$ \frac{N}{2}=32768 $$

Now frequencies are:

$$ {}^{16}C_0,{}^{16}C_1,{}^{16}C_2,\ldots,{}^{16}C_{16} $$

Using symmetry of binomial coefficients:

$$ {}^{16}C_0+{}^{16}C_1+\cdots+{}^{16}C_7 = \frac{1}{2}\left(2^{16}-{}^{16}C_8\right) $$

Since

$$ {}^{16}C_8=12870 $$

we get

$$ {}^{16}C_0+{}^{16}C_1+\cdots+{}^{16}C_7 = \frac{65536-12870}{2} =26333 $$

Now add ${}^{16}C_8$:

$$ 26333+12870=39203 $$

So the cumulative frequency crosses $32768$ at $r=8$.

Hence the median corresponds to

$$ X=8(8-1)=56 $$

Final Answer

$$ \boxed{56} $$

So, the correct option is A.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
The mean deviation about the mean for the data

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline x_i & 5 & 7 & 9 & 10 & 12 & 15 \\ \hline f_i & 8 & 6 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 6 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \text { is equal to: } $$
questions.solution:
For a discrete frequency distribution, the mean deviation about the mean is

$$ \text{M.D. about mean}=\frac{\sum f_i |x_i-\bar{x}|}{\sum f_i} $$

First, find the mean $\bar{x}$.

Step 1: Calculate $\sum f_i$ and $\sum f_i x_i$

$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} \hline x_i & f_i & f_i x_i \\ \hline 5 & 8 & 40 \\ 7 & 6 & 42 \\ 9 & 2 & 18 \\ 10 & 2 & 20 \\ 12 & 2 & 24 \\ 15 & 6 & 90 \\ \hline \end{array} $

Now,

$$ \sum f_i = 8+6+2+2+2+6=26 $$

and

$$ \sum f_i x_i = 40+42+18+20+24+90=234 $$

So, the mean is

$$ \bar{x}=\frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}=\frac{234}{26}=9 $$

Step 2: Calculate $\sum f_i |x_i-\bar{x}|$

Since $\bar{x}=9$, we find $|x_i-9|$:

$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} \hline x_i & f_i & |x_i-9| & f_i|x_i-9| \\ \hline 5 & 8 & 4 & 32 \\ 7 & 6 & 2 & 12 \\ 9 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 10 & 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 12 & 2 & 3 & 6 \\ 15 & 6 & 6 & 36 \\ \hline \end{array} $

Now,

$$ \sum f_i |x_i-\bar{x}| = 32+12+0+2+6+36=88 $$

Step 3: Find mean deviation about the mean

$$ \text{M.D. about mean}=\frac{88}{26}=\frac{44}{13} $$

So, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{44}{13}} $$

Hence, Option C is correct.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
questions.question: Let the mean and the variance of seven observations $2,4, \alpha, 8, \beta, 12,14, \alpha<\beta$, be 8 and 16 respectively. Then the quadratic equation whose roots are $3 \alpha+2$ and $2 \beta+1$ is :
questions.solution:
To find the required quadratic equation, we must first find the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

Given that there are 7 observations: $2, 4, \alpha, 8, \beta, 12, 14$. Let $n = 7$.

The mean of these observations ($\bar{x}$) is $8$.

According to the formula for the mean:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ 8 = \frac{2 + 4 + \alpha + 8 + \beta + 12 + 14}{7} $$

$$ 56 = 40 + \alpha + \beta $$

$$ \alpha + \beta = 16 \quad \text{--- (1)} $$

Next, the variance ($\sigma^2$) is given as $16$. The formula for variance is:

$$ \sigma^2 = \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - (\bar{x})^2 $$

Substituting the known values:

$$ 16 = \frac{2^2 + 4^2 + \alpha^2 + 8^2 + \beta^2 + 12^2 + 14^2}{7} - (8)^2 $$

$$ 16 = \frac{4 + 16 + \alpha^2 + 64 + \beta^2 + 144 + 196}{7} - 64 $$

$$ 16 + 64 = \frac{424 + \alpha^2 + \beta^2}{7} $$

$$ 80 \times 7 = 424 + \alpha^2 + \beta^2 $$

$$ 560 = 424 + \alpha^2 + \beta^2 $$

$$ \alpha^2 + \beta^2 = 136 \quad \text{--- (2)} $$

Now, using the algebraic identity $(\alpha + \beta)^2 = \alpha^2 + \beta^2 + 2\alpha \beta $, we can find the product $\alpha \beta$:

$$ (16)^2 = 136 + 2\alpha \beta $$

$$ 256 = 136 + 2\alpha \beta $$

$$ 2\alpha \beta = 120 $$

$$ \alpha \beta = 60 $$

The numbers $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of the quadratic equation $t^2 - (\alpha + \beta)t + \alpha \beta = 0$:

$$ t^2 - 16t + 60 = 0 $$

$$ t^2 - 10t - 6t + 60 = 0 $$

$$ t(t - 10) - 6(t - 10) = 0 $$

$$ (t - 6)(t - 10) = 0 $$

Thus, the values are $6$ and $10$. Since we are given that $\alpha < \beta$, we have:

$$ \alpha = 6 $$

$$ \beta = 10 $$

We are asked to find the quadratic equation whose roots are $3\alpha + 2$ and $2\beta + 1$. Let's evaluate these roots:

$$ \text{First root} = 3(6) + 2 = 18 + 2 = 20 $$

$$ \text{Second root} = 2(10) + 1 = 20 + 1 = 21 $$

Now, let's find the sum and the product of the roots for our new quadratic equation:

$$ \text{Sum of roots} = 20 + 21 = 41 $$

$$ \text{Product of roots} = 20 \times 21 = 420 $$

The standard form of a quadratic equation is $x^2 - (\text{Sum of roots})x + (\text{Product of roots}) = 0$.

Substituting the values:

$$ x^2 - 41x + 420 = 0 $$

Hence, the correct option is Option B.

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
questions.question: A data consists of 20 observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{20}$. If $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{20}\left(x_i+5\right)^2=2500$ and $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{20}\left(x_i-5\right)^2=100$, then the ratio of mean to standard deviation of this data is :
questions.solution:
Let the mean of the 20 observations be $\bar{x}$ and the standard deviation be $\sigma$.

We are given:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i+5)^2 = 2500 $$

and

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i-5)^2 = 100 $$

We will expand both expressions.

First,

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i+5)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i^2+10x_i+25) $$

$$ = \sum x_i^2 + 10\sum x_i + 20\cdot 25 $$

$$ = \sum x_i^2 + 10\sum x_i + 500 $$

So,

$$ \sum x_i^2 + 10\sum x_i + 500 = 2500 $$

$$ \sum x_i^2 + 10\sum x_i = 2000 \qquad ...(1) $$

Now expand the second one:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i-5)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i^2-10x_i+25) $$

$$ = \sum x_i^2 - 10\sum x_i + 500 $$

So,

$$ \sum x_i^2 - 10\sum x_i + 500 = 100 $$

$$ \sum x_i^2 - 10\sum x_i = -400 \qquad ...(2) $$

Now add (1) and (2):

$$ 2\sum x_i^2 = 1600 $$

$$ \sum x_i^2 = 800 $$

Now subtract (2) from (1):

$$ 20\sum x_i = 2400 $$

$$ \sum x_i = 120 $$

Hence mean:

$$ \bar{x}=\frac{\sum x_i}{20}=\frac{120}{20}=6 $$

Now use the formula for standard deviation:

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum x_i^2}{20} - \bar{x}^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{\frac{800}{20} - 6^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{40-36} $$

$$ = \sqrt{4}=2 $$

Therefore, the ratio of mean to standard deviation is

$$ \bar{x} : \sigma = 6:2 = 3:1 $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{3:1} $$

Option B

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
questions.question: A set of four observations has mean 1 and variance 13. Another set of six observations has mean 2 and variance 1 . Then, the variance of all these 10 observations is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Observation } & \boldsymbol{x}_1 & \boldsymbol{x}_2 & \boldsymbol{x}_3 & \boldsymbol{x}_4 & \boldsymbol{n} & \text { Observation } & \boldsymbol{y}_1 & \boldsymbol{y}_2 & \boldsymbol{y}_3 & \boldsymbol{y}_4 & \boldsymbol{y}_5 & \boldsymbol{y}_6 & \boldsymbol{n} \\ \hline \text { Set } & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 4 & \text { Set } & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 6 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \text { Set } 1 \rightrightarrows \mu_x=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i}{4}=1 \ldots . (i) $$

$\sigma_x^2=\left\lfloor\left(\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i^2}{4}\right)-\mu_x^2\right\rfloor=13 \ldots \ldots(i i)$

Set $2 \rightrightarrows \mu_y=\frac{\sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j}{6}=2 \ldots \ldots(i i i)$

$\sigma_y^2=\left[\left(\frac{\sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j^2}{6}\right)-\mu_y^2\right]=1 \ldots \ldots(i v)$

We mathematically extract from equations $(i)$ and $(i i)$ :

$$ \sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i=4 \ldots .(\boldsymbol{v}) \quad \sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i^2=4\left(\mu_x^2+13\right)=4(1+13)=56 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v} \boldsymbol{i}) $$

We mathematically extract from equations (iii) and (iv) :

$$ \sum\limits_{j=1}^6 \boldsymbol{y}_j=12 \ldots .(\boldsymbol{v i i}) \quad \sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j^2=6\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_y^2+1\right)=6(4+1)=30 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v i i i}) $$

We have mathematically compiled a summary of all the information in equations (v) through (viii) to compute the mean and the variance for the 10 datapoints in set $1 \cup$ set 2 :

$\mu_t=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i+\sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j}{4+6}=\frac{4+12}{10}=1.6 \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{i x})$

$\sigma_t^2=\left[\left(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^4 \boldsymbol{x}_i^2+\sum_{j=1}^6 \boldsymbol{y}_j^2}{4+6}\right)-\boldsymbol{\mu}_t^2\right]$

$=\frac{56+30}{4+6}-\begin{gathered} (1.6)^2\end{gathered}$

$=8.6-2.56=6.04$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift
questions.question: Let a point A lie between the parallel lines $\mathrm{L}_1$ and $\mathrm{L}_2$ such that its distances from $\mathrm{L}_1$ and $\mathrm{L}_2$ are 6 and 3 units, respectively. Then the area (in sq. units) of the equilateral triangle ABC , where the points B and C lie on the lines $\mathrm{L}_1$ and $\mathrm{L}_2$, respectively, is :
questions.solution:
$$ \text { Let } a \text { be the side of } \triangle A B C $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \sin \theta=\frac{3}{a} \\ & \sin \left(60^{\circ}+\theta\right)=\frac{9}{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \sqrt{1-\frac{9}{a^2}}+\frac{3}{a} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{9}{a} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}\left(\sqrt{a^2-9}\right)+3=18 \\ & \Rightarrow 3\left(a^2-9\right)=15^2 \quad \Rightarrow a^2-9=15 \times 5 \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow a^2=84 \\ & \text { Area }=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^2=21 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$
