questions.question: Potential energy ( $V$ ) versus distance ( $x$ ) is given by the graph. Rank various regions as per the magnitudes of the force ( $F$ ) acting on a particle from high to low.
questions.solution:
The force (F) acting on a particle is defined as the negative gradient of its potential energy (V) with respect to position (x) :

$$ \mathrm{F}=-\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}} $$

Here, $\frac{d V}{d x}$ is the slope of the $V$ versus $x$ graph. So, the magnitude of the force $(|F|)$ is the absolute value of the slope in each region :

$$ |\mathrm{F}|=\mid \text { slope of the } \mathrm{V}-\mathrm{x} \text { graph } \mid $$

Using the formula of slope

$$ \text { Slope }=m=\frac{V_2-V_1}{x_2-x_1} $$

For region AB (from $\mathrm{x}=0$ to $\mathrm{x}=1$ ) :

Points are ( 0,0 ) and ( 1,1 )

$$ \left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}}\right|=\left|\frac{1-0}{1-0}\right|=1 \text { unit } $$

For region BC (from $\mathrm{x}=1$ to $\mathrm{x}=2$ ) :

Points are $(1,1)$ and $(2,4)$

$$ \left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{BC}}\right|=\left|\frac{4-1}{2-1}\right|=3 \text { unit } $$

For region CD (from $\mathrm{x}=2$ to $\mathrm{x}=4$ ) :

Points are (2,4) and (4,4)

$$ \left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{CD}}\right|=\left|\frac{4-4}{4-2}\right|=0 \text { unit } $$

For region DE (from $\mathrm{x}=4$ to $\mathrm{x}=6$ ):

Points are $(4,4)$ and $(6,3)$

$$ \left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{DE}}\right|=\left|\frac{3-4}{6-4}\right|=0.5 \text { unit } $$

The descending order of the magnitude of forces is,

$$ 3>1>0.5>0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{BC}}>\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}}>\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{DE}}>\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{CD}} $$

Therefore, region $B C$ has the greatest slope and thus the strongest force, while region CD has no slope and zero force.

Hence, the correct option is (A).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Evening Shift
questions.question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : An object moves from position $r_1$ to position $r_2$ under a conservative force field $\vec{F}$. The work done by the force is $W=-\int\limits_{r_1}^{r_2} \vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}$.

Statement II : Any object moving from one location to another location can follow infinite number of paths. Therefore, the amount of work done by the object changes with the path it follows for a conservative force.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
Statement I is true but Statement II is false

Statement I is false but Statement II is true

Both Statement I and Statement II are false

Both Statement I and Statement II are true
questions.solution:
Let a conservative force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ is acting on an object. For infinitesimally small displacement $\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{dx} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{dy} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{dz} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$, the work done is,

$$ \mathrm{dW}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} $$

The work done (W) by a variable force $\vec{F}$ when displacing an object from an initial position vector $\vec{r}_1$ to a final position vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_2$ is the total work done in moving the object,

The work done is, $W=\int\limits_{r_1}^{r_2} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}$

So, this formula is valid for all forces whether it is conservative or non-conservative force. But the statement I states that the work done is $W=-\int_{r_1}^{r_2} \vec{F}$. $d \vec{r}$. Hence, the statement $I$ is false.

Work done by a conservative force is path independent. And the work done by the conservative force is negative of the change in potential energy, i.e., the work done by the conservative force is stored in object as potential energy.

The work done depends only on the initial and final positions.

$$ \mathrm{W}=\int\limits_{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_1}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~F}} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=-\left(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}\right) $$

For a particle going from point A to point B , there are infinitely many possible paths. So, it is a geometrical fact and is correct.

A force is called conservative if $\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=0$

That means work done in a closed path is zero.

Hence for motion from A to B :

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{~B}}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{B}} $$

So work done depends only on initial position and final position and not on the path followed.

But the statement says, "work done changes with path for a conservative force", so the statement is incorrect because for conservative forces, straight path, curved path, zig-zag path all give the same work between the same two points. Hence, the statement II is incorrect.

Therefore, both Statement I and Statement II are false. Hence, the correct option is (C).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Evening Shift
questions.question: A bead $P$ sliding on a frictionless semi-circular string $(A C B)$ and it is at point $S$ at $t =0$ and at this instant the horizontal component of its velocity is $v$. Another bead $Q$ of the same mass as $P$ is ejected from point $A$ at $t=0$ along the horizontal string $A B$, with the speed $v$, friction between the beads and the respective strings may be neglected in both cases. Let $t_P$ and $t_Q$ be the respective times taken by beads $P$ and $Q$ to reach the point $B$, then the relation between $t_P$ and $t_Q$ is
questions.solution:
For bead Q

Bead $Q$ moves along the horizontal straight string $A B$. The length $A B$ is $2 R$ where $R$ is the radius of semicircular path.

Since the string is frictionless and horizontal, there is no component of gravity acting along the direction of motion.

Bead Q maintains a constant horizontal velocity v throughout the motion.

$$ t_Q=\frac{A B}{v}=2\left[\frac{R}{v}\right] $$

For bead P

Bead P moves along the semi-circular path ACB under the influence of gravity. And along horizontal the net force is zero, i.e., the acceleration along vertical will be non-zero and the acceleration along horizontal will be zero.

At point S , the horizontal component of its velocity is given as v .

As bead P moves from S toward the bottom-most point C , the vertical component of velocity changes but the horizontal component remains same.

When bead P reaches point B then the horizontal displacement is $\mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{OB}$

$$ \mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{R} \cos 45^{\circ} $$

Then the time taken to reach point $B$ is $t_P=\frac{S^{\prime} B}{v}=\frac{R \cos 45^{\circ}+R}{v}=\frac{R}{v}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right]$

So, the ratio of time taken by $Q$ and $P$ to reach $B$ is,

$\frac{t_Q}{t_P}=\frac{2[R / v]}{\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right][R / v]}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}+1}=[8-2 \sqrt{2}]>1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{P}}}>1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}>\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{P}}$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Morning Shift
questions.question: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the object be m and the initial speed be u .

The angle of projection is $\theta=60^{\circ}$.

At point A

The kinetic energy at point $A$ is given as $K$.

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^2=\mathrm{K} $$

At Point B (Maximum Height)

At the highest point of a projectile's path, the vertical component of velocity becomes zero $\left(v_y=0\right)$. The object only possesses the horizontal component of velocity ( $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ).

The horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the motion (assuming no air resistance):

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{u} \cos 60^{\circ}=\mathrm{u}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2} $$

So, the kinetic energy at B is,

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2}\right)^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{4}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^2\right)=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{4}$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{4} $$

At Point C

Point C is at the same horizontal level as point A . By the law of conservation of energy, the speed of the projectile when it hits the ground at the same level is equal to the speed of projection.

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{u} $$

Therefore, the kinetic energy at C is equal to the kinetic energy at A :

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{K} $$

The difference in kinetic energies at B and C :

$$ \Delta K_{B C}=\left|K_C-K_B\right|=\left|K-\frac{K}{4}\right|=\frac{3 K}{4} $$

So, the ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points B and C to that at point A is

Ratio $=\frac{\Delta K_{B C}}{K_A}=\frac{(3 / 4) K}{K}=\frac{3}{4}$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Morning Shift
questions.question: Two blocks with masses 100 g and 200 g are attached to the ends of springs $A$ and $B$ as shown in figure. The energy stored in $A$ is $E$. The energy stored in $B$, when spring constants $k_A, k_B$ of $A$ and $B$, respectively satisfy the relation $4 k_A=3 k_B$, is :
questions.solution:
When a mass $m$ is suspended from a spring of constant $k$, the spring stretches.

If the stretch in spring is $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{kx}=\mathrm{mg} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}$

The potential energy ( $U$ ) stored in the spring is given by :

$$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^2 $$

Substituting $\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}$ :

$$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k}\left(\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}\right)^2=\frac{(\mathrm{mg})^2}{2 \mathrm{k}} $$

Mass of block A is $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{A}}=100 \mathrm{~g}$.

The spring constant is $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}$.

So, the stored energy in spring A is $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{E}$.

Using the formula :

$$ E=\frac{\left(m_A g\right)^2}{2 k_A} $$

Mass of block B is $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}}=200 \mathrm{~g}=2 \times 100 \mathrm{~g}=2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{A}}$

The spring constant of spring B is $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}$.

It is given that $4 \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}=3 \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}$.

So, the stored energy in spring B is

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{~g}\right)^2}{2 \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}$ :

$$ E_B=\frac{\left(2 m_A g\right)^2}{2\left(\frac{4}{3} k_A\right)}$$

$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{4 \times \frac{3}{8} \times\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{g}\right)^2}{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{12}{8} \times \frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{g}\right)^2}{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{g}\right)^2}{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=3 \times\left(\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{g}\right)^2}{2 \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=3 \mathrm{E}\end{gathered}$

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
questions.question:
A spherical ball of mass 2 kg falls from a height of 10 m and is brought to rest after penetrating 10 cm into sand.

The average force exerted by sand on the ball is _________ N.

(Take $g = 10 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$)
questions.solution:
Using the work-energy theorem, the work done by all forces acting on a body is equal to the change in its kinetic energy.

The ball falls from height $\mathrm{h}=10 \mathrm{~m}$ under gravity. And the ball enters the sand and travels a distance $\mathrm{d}= 10 \mathrm{~cm}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ before coming to rest.

Let m be the mass of the ball, h be the initial height, d be the penetration depth, and $\mathrm{F}_{\text {avg }}$ be the average resistive force exerted by the sand.

The total work done on the ball from the moment it is dropped until it stops is:

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\text {total }}=\mathrm{W}_{\text {gravity }}+\mathrm{W}_{\text {sand }} $$

Since the ball starts from rest at height $h$ and ends at rest after depth $d$, the total change in kinetic energy is zero $(\Delta \mathrm{K}=0)$.

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\text {gravity }}+\mathrm{W}_{\text {sand }}=0 $$

Gravity acts over the entire vertical displacement ( $\mathrm{h}+\mathrm{d}$ ).

So, the work done by gravitational force is $\mathrm{W}_{\text {gravity }}=\mathrm{mg}(\mathrm{h}+\mathrm{d})$

The resistive force $F_{\text {avg }}$ acts upwards, opposite to the displacement $d$.

So, the work done by the sand is $\mathrm{W}_{\text {sand }}=-\mathrm{F}_{\text {avg }} \cdot \mathrm{d}$

Substituting these into the energy equation :

$$ \mathrm{mg}(\mathrm{~h}+\mathrm{d})-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{avg}} \cdot \mathrm{~d}=0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{avg}}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}(\mathrm{~h}+\mathrm{d})}{\mathrm{d}} $$

The mass of ball is $\mathrm{m}=2 \mathrm{~kg}$, the height of fall is $\mathrm{h}=10 \mathrm{~m}$, the distance of penetration in sand is $\mathrm{d}=$ 0.1 m , and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{avg}}=\frac{2 \times 10 \times(10+0.1)}{0.1} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $F_{a v g}=\frac{20 \times 10.1}{0.1}=20 \times 101$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{avg}}=2020 \mathrm{~N} $$

Therefore, the average force exerted by sand on the ball is 2020 N .

Hence, the correct option is (B).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg begins to move under the influence of time dependent force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\left(2 \mathrm{t} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right) \mathrm{N}$, where $\hat{\mathrm{i}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ are unit vectors along $x$ and y -axis respectively. The power produced by the force at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ W.
questions.solution:
Power (P) is defined as the rate at which work is done. It can be calculated as the dot product of the force vector $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}})$ and the velocity vector $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}})$.

According to Newton's Second Law $(\vec{F}=m \vec{a})$, the acceleration is :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}}{\mathrm{~m}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{t} \hat{\imath}+6 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{\jmath}}{2} $$

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\left(\mathrm{t} \hat{\imath}+3 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{\jmath}\right) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

Velocity is the integral of acceleration with respect to time. Since the body begins to move, we assume the initial velocity is zero at $\mathrm{t}=0$ :

$$ \vec{v}=\int \vec{a} d t=\int_0^t\left(t \hat{\imath}+3 t^2 \hat{\jmath}\right) d t $$

$$ \vec{v}=\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(\frac{3 t^3}{3}\right) \hat{\jmath}=\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right) \hat{\imath}+t^3 \hat{\jmath} $$

Force at $\mathrm{t}=2$ :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}(2)=[2(2)] \hat{i}+\left[6(2)^2\right] \hat{\jmath}=(4 \hat{i}+24 \hat{\jmath}) \mathrm{N} $$

Velocity at $\mathrm{t}=2$ :

$$ \vec{v}(2)=\left[\frac{(2)^2}{2}\right] \hat{\imath}+(2)^3 \hat{\jmath}=(2 \hat{\imath}+8 \hat{\jmath}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} $$

So, the power at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is,

$$ \mathrm{P}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} $$

$$ \begin{gathered} P=(4 \hat{\imath}+24 \hat{\jmath}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}+8 \hat{\jmath}) \\ P=(4 \times 2)+(24 \times 8) \\ P=8+192=200 \mathrm{~W} \end{gathered} $$

Therefore, the correct answer is 200 .

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
questions.question: A 1 kg block subjected to two simultaneous forces $(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \mathrm{N}$ and $(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \mathrm{N}$ is moved a distance of 25 m along $(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}})$ direction. The work done in this process is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
questions.solution:
When multiple forces act on a body simultaneously, the net force is the vector sum of all individual forces :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_1+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=(2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+(3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=(2+3) \hat{\imath}+(3-1) \hat{\jmath}+(4-2) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{n e t}=(5 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \mathrm{N} $$

The block moves a distance of 25 m along the direction of the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=3 \hat{\imath}-4 \hat{\jmath}$. The unit vector in this direction is :

$$ \hat{u}=\frac{\vec{A}}{|\vec{A}|}=\frac{3 \hat{\imath}-4 \hat{\jmath}}{\sqrt{3^2+(-4)^2}}=\frac{3 \hat{\imath}-4 \hat{\jmath}}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{3 \hat{\imath}-4 \hat{\jmath}}{5} $$

The displacement vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ is

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=25 \times\left(\frac{3 \hat{\imath}-4 \hat{\jmath}}{5}\right)=5(3 \hat{\imath}-4 \hat{\jmath})=(15 \hat{\imath}-20 \hat{\jmath}) \mathrm{m} $$

Work done is defined as the dot product of the force vector and the displacement vector :

$$ \mathrm{W}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~d}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $W=(5 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(15 \hat{\imath}-20 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k})$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=(5 \times 15)+(2 \times(-20))+(2 \times 0)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=75-40+0=35 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the correct answer is 35 .

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
questions.question: A mass of 1 kg is kept on a inclined plane with $30^{\circ}$ inclination with respect to horizontal plane and it is at rest initially. Then the whole assembly is moved up with constant velocity of $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The work done by the frictional force in time 2 s is $\_\_\_\_$ J. (Take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
When the block is at rest on the incline, the forces acting on it are :

$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{mg}$ is weight acting vertically downward.

$\mathrm{N}=$ Normal force acting perpendicular to the incline.

$\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=$ Static friction acting up the incline, preventing the block from sliding down.

Since the block is at rest relative to the incline, the frictional force must balance the component of gravity acting down the slope :

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta $$

The mass of block is $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~kg}$, acceleration due to gravity is $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $\theta=30^{\circ}$

So, value of static friction is

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=1 \times 10 \times \sin 30^{\circ}=10 \times 0.5=5 \mathrm{~N} $$

The friction force is 5 N directed up along the incline.

As the entire assembly moves vertically upward with a constant velocity $\mathrm{v}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, so in time $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$, the vertical displacement $S$ is:

$$ S=v \times t=4 \times 2=8 \mathrm{~m} $$

Work done is defined as $W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}=F d \cos \phi$, where $\phi$ is the angle between the force and the displacement.

The angle between the friction force and the vertical is $90^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=60^{\circ}=\phi$.

So, work done by frictional force is,

$$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}} \times \mathrm{S} \times \cos 60^{\circ} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=5 \times 8 \times 0.5 \mathrm{~J}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=5 \times 4=20 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the work done by the frictional force is 20 J .

Hence, the correct option is (A).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
questions.question: A body of mass 1 kg moves along a straight line with a velocity $v=2 x^2$. The work done by the body during displacement from $x=0$ to 5 m is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
questions.solution:
Work done (W) is defined as the integral of force (F) over a displacement (dx):

$$ \mathrm{W}=\int_{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}}^{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{f}}} \mathrm{Fdx} $$

From Newton's Second Law, $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{ma}$, and acceleration $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}$.

Using the chain rule, $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{v} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}$.

$$ W=\int_{x_i}^{x_f}\left[m\left(v \frac{d v}{d x}\right)\right] d x $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $W=\int_{v_i}^{v_f} m v d v=m \int_{v_i}^{v_f} v d v=m\left[\frac{v^2}{2}\right]_{v_i}^{v_f}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{f}}^2-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{i}}^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} . $$

The mass of the body is $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~kg}$

The velocity of body is given as function of position, $\mathrm{v}=2 \mathrm{x}^2$

Initial position is $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=0 \mathrm{~m}$

Final position is $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{f}}=5 \mathrm{~m}$

At $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=0$ :

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{i}}=2(0)^2=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

At $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{f}}=5$ :

$$ v_f=2(5)^2=2(25)=50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

On substituting these values into the Work-Energy equation :

$$ \mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{f}}^2-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{i}}^2\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times\left(50^2-0^2\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{2} \times(2500)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=1250 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the work done by the body during displacement from $\mathrm{x}=0$ to $\mathrm{x}=5 \mathrm{~m}$ is 1250 J .

Hence, the correct option is (C).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
questions.question: The velocity at which 6 kg mass (shown in figure) strikes the ground when it is released from a height of 6 m above the ground is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. Assume pulley is massless and string is light and inextensible. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
For the 6 kg mass $(\mathrm{M})$ moving down and the 2 kg mass $(\mathrm{m})$ moving up, let the acceleration of system is a and tension in the string is T .

Using Newton's second law ( $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{ma}$ ) of motion for M

$$\mathrm{Mg}-\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{Ma} \ldots (i) $$

For m ,

$$ \mathrm{T}-\mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{ma} \ldots (ii)$$

Adding the two equations :

$$ (M-m) g=(M+m) a $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ a=\frac{(M-m)}{M+m} g $$

Substituting $\mathrm{M}=6, \mathrm{~m}=2$, and $\mathrm{g}=10$ :

$$ \mathrm{a}=\frac{6-2}{6+2} \times 10=\frac{4}{8} \times 10=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

Using the kinematic equation for constant acceleration, starting from rest ( $u=0$ ) :

$$ \mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{as}} $$

Where s is the height $\mathrm{h}=6 \mathrm{~m}$.

$$ v=\sqrt{2 \times 5 \times 6} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{60} \approx 7.745 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Therefore, the velocity is approximately $7.74 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

The correct option is (A).

questions.title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
questions.question:
The rain drop of mass 1 g , starts with zero velocity from a height of 1 km . It hits the ground with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The work done by the unknown resistive force is $\_\_\_\_$ J.

(take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
The Work-Energy Theorem states that the total work done by all forces (conservative and non-conservative) on a particle is equal to the change in its kinetic energy.

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\text {total }}=\Delta \mathrm{K} $$

When rain drop is falling, it experiences gravitational force (vertically downward) and resistive force which opposes motion of rain drop (vertically upward).

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\text {gravity }}+\mathrm{W}_{\text {resistive }}=\mathrm{K}_{\text {final }}-\mathrm{K}_{\text {initial }} $$

Work done by gravity when an object falls a height h is:

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{~h}=\mathrm{mgh} $$

The mass of the rain drop is $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~g}=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$

The height of drop is $\mathrm{h}=1 \mathrm{~km}=1000 \mathrm{~m}$

And the acceleration due to gravity is $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

Putting the values,

$$ W_g=\left(10^{-3}\right) \times(10) \times(1000)=10 \mathrm{~J} $$

The initial velocity is $u=0$ as it started from rest, so the initial kinetic energy is $K_{\text {initial }}=\frac{1}{2} m u^2=0$.

The final velocity when rain drop hits the ground is $\mathrm{v}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Thus, the final kinetic energy of the rain drop is

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{final}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $K_{f i n a l}=\frac{1}{2} \times\left(10^{-3}\right) \times(5)^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{K}_{\text {final }}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3} \times 25=12.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}=0.0125 \mathrm{~J} $$

Using the work-energy theorem equation,

$$ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{final}}-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{initial}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $10 \mathrm{~J}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{r}}=0.0125 \mathrm{~J}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{r}}=0.0125-10$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{r}}=-9.9875 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the magnitude of the work done by the resistive force is approximately 9.98 J .

Thus, the correct option is (D).
